infobox.value: ([/ˈaɪ.əwə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-Iowa.ogg "File:En-us-Iowa.ogg") [*EYE-ə-wə*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"))[[8]](#cite_note-9)[[9]](#cite_note-10)[[10]](#cite_note-11) is a landlocked [state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") in the upper [Midwestern](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States") region of the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"). It borders the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") to the east and the [Missouri River](/wiki/Missouri_River "Missouri River") and [Big Sioux River](/wiki/Big_Sioux_River "Big Sioux River") to the west; [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin") to the northeast, [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois") to the east and southeast, [Missouri](/wiki/Missouri "Missouri") to the south, [Nebraska](/wiki/Nebraska "Nebraska") to the west, [South Dakota](/wiki/South_Dakota "South Dakota") to the northwest, and [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota "Minnesota") to the north. Iowa is the [26th largest](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_area "List of U.S. states and territories by area") in total area and the [31st most populous](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_population "List of U.S. states and territories by population") of the [50 U.S. states](/wiki/List_of_states_and_territories_of_the_United_States "List of states and territories of the United States"), with a population of 3.19 million.[[11]](#cite_note-12) The state's [capital](/wiki/List_of_capitals_in_the_United_States "List of capitals in the United States"), [most populous city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Iowa "List of cities in Iowa"), and largest [metropolitan area](/wiki/List_of_metropolitan_statistical_areas "List of metropolitan statistical areas") fully located within the state is [Des Moines](/wiki/Des_Moines,_Iowa "Des Moines, Iowa").

**Iowa** ([/ˈaɪ.əwə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-Iowa.ogg "File:En-us-Iowa.ogg") [*EYE-ə-wə*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"))[[8]](#cite_note-9)[[9]](#cite_note-10)[[10]](#cite_note-11) is a landlocked [state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") in the upper [Midwestern](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States") region of the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"). It borders the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") to the east and the [Missouri River](/wiki/Missouri_River "Missouri River") and [Big Sioux River](/wiki/Big_Sioux_River "Big Sioux River") to the west; [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin") to the northeast, [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois") to the east and southeast, [Missouri](/wiki/Missouri "Missouri") to the south, [Nebraska](/wiki/Nebraska "Nebraska") to the west, [South Dakota](/wiki/South_Dakota "South Dakota") to the northwest, and [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota "Minnesota") to the north. Iowa is the [26th largest](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_area "List of U.S. states and territories by area") in total area and the [31st most populous](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_population "List of U.S. states and territories by population") of the [50 U.S. states](/wiki/List_of_states_and_territories_of_the_United_States "List of states and territories of the United States"), with a population of 3.19 million.[[11]](#cite_note-12) The state's [capital](/wiki/List_of_capitals_in_the_United_States "List of capitals in the United States"), [most populous city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Iowa "List of cities in Iowa"), and largest [metropolitan area](/wiki/List_of_metropolitan_statistical_areas "List of metropolitan statistical areas") fully located within the state is [Des Moines](/wiki/Des_Moines,_Iowa "Des Moines, Iowa").

During the 18th and early 19th centuries, Iowa was a part of [French Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana_(New_France) "Louisiana (New France)") and [Spanish Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana_(New_Spain) "Louisiana (New Spain)"); its [state flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Iowa "Flag of Iowa") is patterned after the [flag of France](/wiki/Flag_of_France "Flag of France"). After the [Louisiana Purchase](/wiki/Louisiana_Purchase "Louisiana Purchase"), pioneers laid the foundation for an agriculture-based economy in the heart of the [Corn Belt](/wiki/Corn_Belt "Corn Belt").[[12]](#cite_note-13) In the latter half of the 20th century, Iowa's agricultural economy began to transition to a diversified economy of advanced manufacturing, processing, [financial services](/wiki/Financial_services "Financial services"), [information technology](/wiki/Information_technology "Information technology"), [biotechnology](/wiki/Biotechnology "Biotechnology"), and [green energy](/wiki/Sustainable_energy "Sustainable energy") production.[[13]](#cite_note-iaindustries-14)[[14]](#cite_note-energy.iastate.edu-15)

Politically, Iowa is notable for the [Iowa Caucuses](/wiki/Iowa_caucuses "Iowa caucuses"), an influential event in national politics, as well as its high levels of [voter turnout](/wiki/Voter_turnout_in_United_States_presidential_elections "Voter turnout in United States presidential elections") and foundational leadership in civil rights including early adoption or support of [black suffrage](/wiki/Black_suffrage "Black suffrage").[[15]](#cite_note-16)[[16]](#cite_note-17)

## Etymology

Like [many other states](/wiki/List_of_state_and_territory_name_etymologies_of_the_United_States "List of state and territory name etymologies of the United States"), Iowa takes its name from its predecessor, [Iowa Territory](/wiki/Iowa_Territory "Iowa Territory"), whose name in turn is derived from the [Iowa River](/wiki/Iowa_River "Iowa River"), and ultimately from the [ethnonym](/wiki/Ethnonym "Ethnonym") of the indigenous [Ioway people](/wiki/Ioway_people "Ioway people").
The Ioway are a [Chiwere](/wiki/Chiwere_language "Chiwere language")-speaking [Siouan Nation](/wiki/Sioux "Sioux"), who were once part of the [Ho-Chunk](/wiki/Ho-Chunk "Ho-Chunk") Confederation that inhabited the area now corresponding to several [Midwest](/wiki/Midwest "Midwest") states. The Ioway were one of the many [Native American](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") nations whose territory comprised the future state of Iowa before the time of European colonization.[[17]](#cite_note-18)

## History

Main article: [History of Iowa](/wiki/History_of_Iowa "History of Iowa")

See also: [Native American tribes in Iowa](/wiki/Native_American_tribes_in_Iowa "Native American tribes in Iowa")

### Prehistory

Main articles: [Iowa archaeology](/wiki/Iowa_archaeology "Iowa archaeology") and [American Indians of Iowa](/wiki/American_Indians_of_Iowa "American Indians of Iowa")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Iowa_archaeology_edgewater.JPG/250px-Iowa_archaeology_edgewater.JPG)](/wiki/File:Iowa_archaeology_edgewater.JPG)

Excavation of the 3,800-year-old [Edgewater Park Site](/wiki/Edgewater_Park_Site "Edgewater Park Site")

When [Indigenous peoples of the Americas](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") first arrived in what is now Iowa more than 13,000 years ago, they were hunters and gatherers living in a [Pleistocene](/wiki/Pleistocene "Pleistocene") glacial landscape. By the time European explorers and traders visited Iowa, Native Americans were largely settled farmers with complex economic, social, and political systems. This transformation happened gradually. During the [Archaic period](/wiki/Archaic_period_in_North_America "Archaic period in North America") (10,500 to 2,800 years ago), Native Americans adapted to local environments and ecosystems, slowly becoming more sedentary as populations increased.[[18]](#cite_note-iaarch-19)

More than 3,000 years ago, during the [Late Archaic period](/wiki/Late_Archaic_period "Late Archaic period"), Native Americans in Iowa began utilizing domesticated plants. The subsequent [Woodland period](/wiki/Woodland_period "Woodland period") saw an increased reliance on agriculture and social complexity, with increased use of mounds, ceramics, and specialized subsistence. During the Late Prehistoric period (beginning about AD 900) increased use of maize and social changes led to social flourishing and nucleated settlements.[[18]](#cite_note-iaarch-19)

The arrival of European trade goods and diseases in the Protohistoric period led to dramatic population shifts and economic and social upheaval, with the arrival of new tribes and early European explorers and traders. There were numerous native American tribes living in Iowa at the time of early European exploration. Tribes which were probably descendants of the prehistoric [Oneota](/wiki/Oneota "Oneota") include the [Dakota](/wiki/Sioux "Sioux"), [Ho-Chunk](/wiki/Ho-Chunk "Ho-Chunk"), [Ioway](/wiki/Ioway "Ioway"), and [Otoe](/wiki/Otoe_(tribe) "Otoe (tribe)"). Tribes which arrived in Iowa in the late prehistoric or protohistoric periods include the [Illiniwek](/wiki/Illiniwek "Illiniwek"), [Meskwaki](/wiki/Meskwaki "Meskwaki"), [Omaha](/wiki/Omaha_(tribe) "Omaha (tribe)"), and [Sauk](/wiki/Sauk_people "Sauk people").[[18]](#cite_note-iaarch-19)

### Early colonization and trade, 1673–1808

Main articles: [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France"), [Louisiana (New France)](/wiki/Louisiana_(New_France) "Louisiana (New France)"), [French and Indian War](/wiki/French_and_Indian_War "French and Indian War"), [Treaty of Paris (1763)](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1763) "Treaty of Paris (1763)"), [New Spain](/wiki/New_Spain "New Spain"), [Louisiana (New Spain)](/wiki/Louisiana_(New_Spain) "Louisiana (New Spain)"), [Treaty of Aranjuez (1801)](/wiki/Treaty_of_Aranjuez_(1801) "Treaty of Aranjuez (1801)"), [Louisiana Purchase](/wiki/Louisiana_Purchase "Louisiana Purchase"), [District of Louisiana](/wiki/District_of_Louisiana "District of Louisiana"), and [Louisiana Territory](/wiki/Louisiana_Territory "Louisiana Territory")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Iowa_1718.jpg/500px-Iowa_1718.jpg)](/wiki/File:Iowa_1718.jpg)

Iowa in 1718 with the modern state area highlighted

The first known European explorers to document Iowa were [Jacques Marquette](/wiki/Jacques_Marquette "Jacques Marquette") and [Louis Jolliet](/wiki/Louis_Jolliet "Louis Jolliet") who traveled the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") in 1673 documenting several Indigenous villages on the Iowa side.[[19]](#cite_note-peterson2009-20)[[20]](#cite_note-21) The area of Iowa was claimed for France and remained a French territory until 1763. The French, before their impending defeat in the [French and Indian War](/wiki/French_and_Indian_War "French and Indian War"), transferred ownership to their ally, Spain.[[21]](#cite_note-22) Spain practiced very loose control over the Iowa region, granting trading licenses to French and British traders, who established trading posts along the [Mississippi](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") and [Des Moines Rivers](/wiki/Des_Moines_River "Des Moines River").[[19]](#cite_note-peterson2009-20)

Iowa was part of a territory known as [*La Louisiane*](/wiki/Louisiana_(New_France) "Louisiana (New France)") or [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana "Louisiana"), and European traders were interested in lead and furs obtained by Indigenous people. The [Sauk](/wiki/Sauk_people "Sauk people") and [Meskwaki](/wiki/Meskwaki "Meskwaki") effectively controlled trade on the Mississippi in the late 18th century and early 19th century. Among the early traders on the Mississippi were [Julien Dubuque](/wiki/Julien_Dubuque "Julien Dubuque"), [Robert de la Salle](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9-Robert_Cavelier,_Sieur_de_La_Salle "René-Robert Cavelier, Sieur de La Salle"), and [Paul Marin](/wiki/Paul_Marin_de_la_Malgue "Paul Marin de la Malgue").[[19]](#cite_note-peterson2009-20) Along the [Missouri River](/wiki/Missouri_River "Missouri River") at least five French and English trading houses were built before 1808.[[22]](#cite_note-carlson2009-23) In 1800, [Napoleon Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon_Bonaparte "Napoleon Bonaparte") took control of Louisiana from Spain in a [treaty](/wiki/Third_Treaty_of_San_Ildefonso "Third Treaty of San Ildefonso").[[23]](#cite_note-24)

After the 1803 [Louisiana Purchase](/wiki/Louisiana_Purchase "Louisiana Purchase"), Congress divided the Louisiana Purchase into two parts—the Territory of Orleans and the District of Louisiana, with present-day Iowa falling in the latter. The [Indiana Territory](/wiki/Indiana_Territory "Indiana Territory"), created in 1800, exercised jurisdiction over this portion of the District; [William Henry Harrison](/wiki/William_Henry_Harrison "William Henry Harrison") was its first governor. Much of Iowa was mapped by [Zebulon Pike](/wiki/Zebulon_Pike "Zebulon Pike") in 1805,[[24]](#cite_note-25) but it was not until the construction of [Fort Madison](/wiki/Fort_Madison,_Iowa "Fort Madison, Iowa") in 1808 that the U.S. established tenuous military control over the region.[[25]](#cite_note-mckusick2009-26)

### War of 1812 and unstable U.S. control

Main article: [Missouri Territory](/wiki/Missouri_Territory "Missouri Territory")

[Fort Madison](/wiki/Fort_Madison,_Iowa "Fort Madison, Iowa") was built to control trade and establish U.S. dominance over the Upper Mississippi, but it was poorly designed and disliked by the Sauk and Meskwaki, many of whom allied with the British, who had not abandoned claims to the territory.[[25]](#cite_note-mckusick2009-26)[[26]](#cite_note-27) [Fort Madison](/wiki/Fort_Madison,_Iowa "Fort Madison, Iowa") was defeated by British-supported Indigenous people in 1813 during the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812"), and [Fort Shelby](/wiki/Fort_Shelby_(Wisconsin) "Fort Shelby (Wisconsin)") in [Prairie du Chien, Wisconsin](/wiki/Prairie_du_Chien,_Wisconsin "Prairie du Chien, Wisconsin"), also fell to the British. [Black Hawk](/wiki/Black_Hawk_(Sauk_leader) "Black Hawk (Sauk leader)") took part in the siege of Fort Madison.[[27]](#cite_note-28)[[28]](#cite_note-29) Another small military outpost was established along the Mississippi River in present-day [Bellevue](/wiki/Bellevue,_Iowa "Bellevue, Iowa"). This poorly situated stockade was similarly attacked by hundreds of Indigenous people in 1813, but was successfully defended and later abandoned until settlers returned to the area in the mid-1830s.[[29]](#cite_note-30)

After the war, the U.S. re-established control of the region through the construction of [Fort Armstrong](/wiki/Fort_Armstrong,_Illinois "Fort Armstrong, Illinois"), [Fort Snelling](/wiki/Fort_Snelling "Fort Snelling") in [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota "Minnesota"), and [Fort Atkinson](/wiki/Fort_Atkinson_(Nebraska) "Fort Atkinson (Nebraska)") in [Nebraska](/wiki/Nebraska "Nebraska").[[30]](#cite_note-iowaforts-31)

### Indian removal, 1814–1832

See also: [Indian removal](/wiki/Indian_removal "Indian removal")

The United States encouraged settlement of the east side of the Mississippi and removal of Indians to the west.[[31]](#cite_note-32) A disputed [1804 treaty](/wiki/Treaty_of_St._Louis_(1804) "Treaty of St. Louis (1804)") between [Quashquame](/wiki/Quashquame "Quashquame") and [William Henry Harrison](/wiki/William_Henry_Harrison "William Henry Harrison") (then governor of the [Indiana Territory](/wiki/Indiana_Territory "Indiana Territory")) that surrendered much of [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois") to the U.S. enraged many Sauk and led to the 1832 [Black Hawk War](/wiki/Black_Hawk_War "Black Hawk War").[[32]](#cite_note-Jung-33)

The [Sauk](/wiki/Sauk_people "Sauk people") and [Meskwaki](/wiki/Meskwaki "Meskwaki") were forced to sell some of their land in the [Mississippi Valley](/wiki/Mississippi_Valley "Mississippi Valley") to the U.S. in 1832 in the [Black Hawk Purchase](/wiki/Black_Hawk_Purchase "Black Hawk Purchase") Treaty[[33]](#cite_note-34) and sold their remaining land in Iowa in 1842, most of them moving to a reservation in Kansas.[[32]](#cite_note-Jung-33) In 1837, some the [Potawatomi](/wiki/Potawatomi "Potawatomi") from Illinois were resettled in Iowa, while many Meskwaki later returned to Iowa and settled near [Tama, Iowa](/wiki/Tama,_Iowa "Tama, Iowa"); the [Meskwaki Settlement](/wiki/Meskwaki_Settlement "Meskwaki Settlement") remains to this day.[[34]](#cite_note-35) In 1856 the Iowa Legislature passed an unprecedented act allowing the Meskwaki to purchase the land.[[35]](#cite_note-36) The federal government, in contrast, used treaties to force the [Ho-Chunk](/wiki/Ho-Chunk "Ho-Chunk") and the [Dakota](/wiki/Sioux "Sioux") from Iowa by 1848[[36]](#cite_note-37) and 1858,[[37]](#cite_note-38) respectively. Western Iowa around modern [Council Bluffs](/wiki/Council_Bluffs "Council Bluffs") was used as an Indian Reservation for members of the [Council of Three Fires](/wiki/Council_of_Three_Fires "Council of Three Fires").[[38]](#cite_note-39)

### U.S. settlement and statehood, 1832–1860

Main articles: [Michigan Territory](/wiki/Michigan_Territory "Michigan Territory"), [Wisconsin Territory](/wiki/Wisconsin_Territory "Wisconsin Territory"), [Organic act § List of organic acts](/wiki/Organic_act#List_of_organic_acts "Organic act"), [Iowa Territory](/wiki/Iowa_Territory "Iowa Territory"), [Admission to the Union](/wiki/Admission_to_the_Union "Admission to the Union"), and [List of U.S. states by date of admission to the Union](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_by_date_of_admission_to_the_Union "List of U.S. states by date of admission to the Union")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Iowa_territorial_seal.jpg/250px-Iowa_territorial_seal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Iowa_territorial_seal.jpg)

Iowa [Territorial Seal](/wiki/Seal_of_Iowa#Iowa_territorial_seal "Seal of Iowa")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Bellevue%2C_Iowa_in_1848.png/330px-Bellevue%2C_Iowa_in_1848.png)](/wiki/File:Bellevue,_Iowa_in_1848.png)

[Bellevue](/wiki/Bellevue,_Iowa "Bellevue, Iowa") along the Mississippi, 1848

The first American settlers officially moved to Iowa in June 1833.[[39]](#cite_note-Settlers-40) Primarily, they were families from [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio"), [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"), [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), [Indiana](/wiki/Indiana "Indiana"), [Kentucky](/wiki/Kentucky "Kentucky"), and [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia") who settled along the western banks of the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River"), founding the modern day cities of [Dubuque](/wiki/Dubuque,_Iowa "Dubuque, Iowa") and [Bellevue](/wiki/Bellevue,_Iowa "Bellevue, Iowa") near the site of Julien Dubuque's 1785–1810 lead mining operation.[[39]](#cite_note-Settlers-40)[[40]](#cite_note-41)[[41]](#cite_note-42) On July 4, 1838, the [U.S. Congress](/wiki/U.S._Congress "U.S. Congress") established the [Territory of Iowa](/wiki/Territory_of_Iowa "Territory of Iowa"). President [Martin Van Buren](/wiki/Martin_Van_Buren "Martin Van Buren") appointed [Robert Lucas](/wiki/Robert_Lucas_(governor) "Robert Lucas (governor)") governor of the territory, which at the time had 22 counties and a population of 23,242.[[42]](#cite_note-43)

Almost immediately after Iowa achieved territorial status, a clamor arose for statehood. On December 28, 1846, Iowa became the 29th state in the Union when President [James K. Polk](/wiki/James_K._Polk "James K. Polk") signed its admission bill into law. Once admitted to the Union, with the state's boundary issues resolved and most of its land purchased from Natives, Iowa set its direction to development and organized campaigns for settlers and investors, boasting the young frontier state's rich farmlands, fine citizens, free and open society, and good government.[[43]](#cite_note-44)

Iowa has a long tradition of state and county fairs. The first and second [Iowa State Fairs](/wiki/Iowa_State_Fair "Iowa State Fair") were held in the more developed eastern part of the state at [Fairfield](/wiki/Fairfield,_Iowa "Fairfield, Iowa"). The first fair was held October 25–27, 1854, at a cost of around $323. Thereafter, the fair moved to locations closer to the center of the state and in 1886 found a permanent home in Des Moines. The State Fair has been held annually since then, except for a few exceptions: 1898 due to the [Spanish–American War](/wiki/Spanish%E2%80%93American_War "Spanish–American War") and the [World's Fair](/wiki/World%27s_Fair "World's Fair") being held in nearby [Omaha, Nebraska](/wiki/Omaha,_Nebraska "Omaha, Nebraska"); from 1942 to 1945, due to [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), as the fairgrounds were being used as an army supply depot; and in 2020 due to the [COVID pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_States "COVID-19 pandemic in the United States").[[44]](#cite_note-45)[[45]](#cite_note-46)

### Civil War, 1861–1865

Main article: [Iowa in the American Civil War](/wiki/Iowa_in_the_American_Civil_War "Iowa in the American Civil War")

Iowa supported the Union during the [Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), voting heavily for [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln"), though there was an antiwar "[Copperhead](/wiki/Copperheads_(politics) "Copperheads (politics)")" movement in the state, caused partially by a drop in crop prices caused by the war.[[46]](#cite_note-47) There were no battles in the state, although the [Battle of Athens](/wiki/Battle_of_Athens_(1861) "Battle of Athens (1861)"), Missouri, 1861, was fought just across the Des Moines River from [Croton, Iowa](/wiki/Croton,_Iowa "Croton, Iowa"), and shots from the battle landed in Iowa. Iowa sent large supplies of food to the armies and the eastern cities.[[47]](#cite_note-Iowa_Official_Register_page_315-48)

Much of Iowa's support for the Union can be attributed to [Samuel J. Kirkwood](/wiki/Samuel_J._Kirkwood "Samuel J. Kirkwood"), its first wartime governor. Of a total population of 675,000, about 116,000 men were subjected to military duty. Iowa contributed proportionately more soldiers to Civil War military service than did any other state, north or south, sending more than 75,000 volunteers to the armed forces, over one-sixth of whom were killed before the [Confederates](/wiki/Confederate_States_of_America "Confederate States of America") surrendered at [Appomattox](/wiki/Battle_of_Appomattox_Courthouse "Battle of Appomattox Courthouse").[[47]](#cite_note-Iowa_Official_Register_page_315-48)

Most fought in the great campaigns in the [Mississippi Valley](/wiki/Mississippi_Valley "Mississippi Valley") and in the [South](/wiki/American_South "American South").[[48]](#cite_note-49) Iowa troops fought at Wilson's Creek in [Missouri](/wiki/Missouri "Missouri"), [Pea Ridge](/wiki/Battle_of_Pea_Ridge "Battle of Pea Ridge") in [Arkansas](/wiki/Arkansas "Arkansas"), Forts Henry and Donelson, Shiloh, Chattanooga, Chickamauga, Missionary Ridge, and Rossville Gap as well as Vicksburg, Iuka, and Corinth. They served with the Army of the Potomac in [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia") and fought under Union General [Philip Sheridan](/wiki/Philip_Sheridan "Philip Sheridan") in the [Shenandoah Valley](/wiki/Shenandoah_Valley "Shenandoah Valley"). Many died and were buried at Andersonville. They marched on General [Nathaniel Banks](/wiki/Nathaniel_Banks "Nathaniel Banks")' ill-starred expedition to the Red River. Twenty-seven [Iowans](/wiki/Iowans "Iowans") have been awarded the [Medal of Honor](/wiki/Medal_of_Honor "Medal of Honor"), the highest military decoration awarded by the United States government, which was first awarded in the Civil War.[[49]](#cite_note-50)

Iowa had several brigadier generals and four major generals—[Grenville Mellen Dodge](/wiki/Grenville_Mellen_Dodge "Grenville Mellen Dodge"), [Samuel R. Curtis](/wiki/Samuel_R._Curtis "Samuel R. Curtis"), [Francis J. Herron](/wiki/Francis_J._Herron "Francis J. Herron"), and [Frederick Steele](/wiki/Frederick_Steele "Frederick Steele")—and saw many of its generals go on to state and national prominence following the war.[[47]](#cite_note-Iowa_Official_Register_page_315-48)

### Agricultural expansion, 1865–1930

Following the Civil War, Iowa's population continued to grow dramatically, from 674,913 people in 1860[[50]](#cite_note-51) to 1,624,615 in 1880.[[51]](#cite_note-52) The American Civil War briefly brought higher profits.[[52]](#cite_note-ec-ag-53)

In 1917, the United States entered World War I and farmers as well as all Iowans experienced a wartime economy. For farmers, the change was significant. Since the beginning of the war in 1914, Iowa farmers had experienced economic prosperity, which lasted until the end of the war.[[52]](#cite_note-ec-ag-53) In the economic sector, Iowa also has undergone considerable change. Beginning with the first industries developed in the 1830s,[[53]](#cite_note-54) which were mainly for processing materials grown in the area,[[54]](#cite_note-55) Iowa has experienced a gradual increase in the number of business and manufacturing operations.

### Depression, World War II and manufacturing, 1930–1985

The transition from an agricultural economy to a mixed economy happened slowly. The [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") and World War II accelerated the shift away from [smallholder](/wiki/Smallholding "Smallholding") farming to larger farms, and began a trend of urbanization. The period after World War II witnessed a particular increase in manufacturing operations.[[55]](#cite_note-56)

In 1975, Governor Robert D. Ray petitioned President Ford to allow Iowa to accept and resettle [Tai Dam](/wiki/Tai_Dam "Tai Dam") refugees fleeing the Indochina War.[[56]](#cite_note-57) An exception was required for this resettlement as State Dept policy at the time forbid resettlement of large groups of refugees in concentrated communities; an exception was ultimately granted and 1200 Tai Dam were resettled in Iowa. Since then Iowa has accepted thousands of refugees from Laos, Cambodia, Thailand, Bhutan, and Burma.[[57]](#cite_note-58)

The [farm crisis of the 1980s](/wiki/Farm_crisis_of_the_1980s "Farm crisis of the 1980s") caused a major recession in Iowa, causing poverty not seen since the Depression.[[58]](#cite_note-59) The crisis spurred a major, decade-long population decline.[[59]](#cite_note-iwin.iwd.state.ia.us-60)

### Reemergence as a mixed economy, 1985–present

After bottoming out in the 1980s, Iowa's economy began to reduce its dependence on agriculture. By the early 21st century, it was characterized by a mix of manufacturing, biotechnology, finance and insurance services, and government services.[[60]](#cite_note-iowalifechanging.com-61) The population of Iowa has increased at a slower rate than the U.S. as a whole since at least the [1900 census](/wiki/1900_census "1900 census"),[[61]](#cite_note-Census-2021-62) though Iowa now has a predominantly urban population.[[62]](#cite_note-data.iowadatacenter.org-63) The Iowa Economic Development Authority, created in 2011 has replaced the Iowa Department of Economic Development and its annual reports are a source of economic information.[[63]](#cite_note-IEDA-64)

## Geography

Main article: [Geography of Iowa](/wiki/Geography_of_Iowa "Geography of Iowa")

### Boundaries

See also: [List of counties in Iowa](/wiki/List_of_counties_in_Iowa "List of counties in Iowa")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Iowa_topography.jpg/250px-Iowa_topography.jpg)](/wiki/File:Iowa_topography.jpg)

Topography of Iowa, with counties and major streams

Iowa is bordered by the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") on the east along with the [Missouri River](/wiki/Missouri_River "Missouri River") and the [Big Sioux River](/wiki/Big_Sioux_River "Big Sioux River") on the west. The northern boundary is a line along 43 degrees, 30 minutes north latitude.[[64]](#cite_note-65)[[b]](#cite_note-66) The southern border is the [Des Moines River](/wiki/Des_Moines_River "Des Moines River") and a not-quite-straight line along approximately 40 degrees 35 minutes north, as decided by the [U.S. Supreme Court](/wiki/U.S._Supreme_Court "U.S. Supreme Court") in *[Missouri v. Iowa](/wiki/State_of_Missouri_v._State_of_Iowa_(1849) "State of Missouri v. State of Iowa (1849)")* (1849) after a standoff between [Missouri](/wiki/Missouri "Missouri") and Iowa known as the [Honey War](/wiki/Honey_War "Honey War").[[65]](#cite_note-67)[[66]](#cite_note-68)

Iowa is the only state whose east and west borders are formed almost entirely by rivers.[[67]](#cite_note-69) [Carter Lake, Iowa](/wiki/Carter_Lake,_Iowa "Carter Lake, Iowa"), is the only city in the state located west of the Missouri River.[[68]](#cite_note-70)

Iowa has 99 [counties](/wiki/County_(United_States) "County (United States)"), but 100 [county seats](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") because [Lee County](/wiki/Lee_County,_Iowa "Lee County, Iowa") has two. The state capital, [Des Moines](/wiki/Des_Moines "Des Moines"), is in [Polk County](/wiki/Polk_County,_Iowa "Polk County, Iowa").[[69]](#cite_note-71)

### Geology and terrain

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Desoto_Lake1.jpg/250px-Desoto_Lake1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Desoto_Lake1.jpg)

[DeSoto Lake](/wiki/DeSoto_Lake_(Iowa) "DeSoto Lake (Iowa)") at [DeSoto National Wildlife Refuge](/wiki/DeSoto_National_Wildlife_Refuge "DeSoto National Wildlife Refuge")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Iowa_terrain.jpg/250px-Iowa_terrain.jpg)](/wiki/File:Iowa_terrain.jpg)

Iowa terrain

Iowa's bedrock geology generally decreases in age from east to west. In northwest Iowa, [Cretaceous](/wiki/Cretaceous "Cretaceous") bedrock can be 74 million years old; in eastern Iowa [Cambrian](/wiki/Cambrian "Cambrian") bedrock dates to c. 500 million years ago.[[70]](#cite_note-72) The oldest radiometrically dated bedrock in the state is the 2.9 billion year old Otter Creek Layered Mafic Complex. [Precambrian](/wiki/Precambrian "Precambrian") rock is exposed only in the northwest of the state.[[71]](#cite_note-73)

Iowa can be divided into eight [landforms](/wiki/Landform "Landform") based on [glaciation](/wiki/Glaciation "Glaciation"), [soils](/wiki/Soil "Soil"), [topography](/wiki/Topography "Topography"), and river drainage.[[72]](#cite_note-Prior91-74) [Loess](/wiki/Loess "Loess") hills lie along the western border of the state, some of which are several hundred feet thick.[[73]](#cite_note-75) Northeast Iowa along the [Upper Mississippi River](/wiki/Upper_Mississippi_River "Upper Mississippi River") is part of the [Driftless Area](/wiki/Driftless_Area "Driftless Area"), consisting of steep hills and valleys which appear as mountainous.[[74]](#cite_note-76)

Several natural lakes exist, most notably [Spirit Lake](/wiki/Spirit_Lake_(Iowa) "Spirit Lake (Iowa)"), [West Okoboji Lake](/wiki/West_Okoboji_Lake "West Okoboji Lake"), and [East Okoboji Lake](/wiki/East_Okoboji_Lake "East Okoboji Lake") in northwest Iowa (*see [Iowa Great Lakes](/wiki/Iowa_Great_Lakes "Iowa Great Lakes")*). To the east lies [Clear Lake](/wiki/Clear_Lake_(Iowa) "Clear Lake (Iowa)"). Man-made lakes include Lake Odessa,[[75]](#cite_note-Odessa-77) [Saylorville Lake](/wiki/Saylorville_Lake "Saylorville Lake"), [Lake Red Rock](/wiki/Lake_Red_Rock_(Des_Moines_River) "Lake Red Rock (Des Moines River)"), [Coralville Lake](/wiki/Coralville_Lake "Coralville Lake"), Lake MacBride, and Rathbun Lake. Before European settlement, 4 to 6 million acres of the state was covered with wetlands; about 95% of these wetlands have since been drained.[[76]](#cite_note-78)

### Ecology and environment

Main article: [Environment of Iowa](/wiki/Environment_of_Iowa "Environment of Iowa")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Landforms_of_Iowa.jpg/500px-Landforms_of_Iowa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Landforms_of_Iowa.jpg)

Landforms of Iowa

Iowa's natural vegetation is [tallgrass prairie](/wiki/Tallgrass_prairie "Tallgrass prairie") and [savanna](/wiki/Savanna "Savanna") in upland areas, with dense forest and wetlands in flood plains and protected river valleys, and pothole wetlands in northern prairie areas.[[72]](#cite_note-Prior91-74) Most of Iowa is used for agriculture; crops cover 60% of the state, grasslands (mostly pasture and hay with some prairie and wetland) cover 30%, and forests cover 7%; urban areas and water cover another 1% each.[[77]](#cite_note-79)

The southern part of Iowa is categorized as the [Central forest-grasslands transition](/wiki/Central_forest-grasslands_transition "Central forest-grasslands transition") ecoregion.[[78]](#cite_note-80) The Northern, drier part of Iowa is categorized as part of the [Central tall grasslands](/wiki/Central_tall_grasslands "Central tall grasslands").[[79]](#cite_note-81)

There is a dearth of natural areas in Iowa; less than 1% of the [tallgrass prairie](/wiki/Tallgrass_prairie "Tallgrass prairie") that once covered most of Iowa remains intact; only about 5% of the state's prairie pothole wetlands remain, and most of the original forest has been lost.[[80]](#cite_note-82) As of 2005[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Iowa&action=edit) Iowa ranked 49th of U.S. states in public land holdings.[[81]](#cite_note-83) Threatened or endangered animals in Iowa include the [interior least tern](/wiki/Least_tern "Least tern"), [piping plover](/wiki/Piping_plover "Piping plover"), [Indiana bat](/wiki/Indiana_bat "Indiana bat"), [pallid sturgeon](/wiki/Pallid_sturgeon "Pallid sturgeon"), the [Iowa Pleistocene land snail](/wiki/Discus_macclintocki "Discus macclintocki"), [Higgins' eye pearly mussel](/wiki/Higgins%27_eye_pearly_mussel "Higgins' eye pearly mussel"), and the [Topeka shiner](/wiki/Topeka_shiner "Topeka shiner").[[82]](#cite_note-84) Endangered or threatened plants include [western prairie fringed orchid](/wiki/Western_prairie_fringed_orchid "Western prairie fringed orchid"), [eastern prairie fringed orchid](/wiki/Eastern_prairie_fringed_orchid "Eastern prairie fringed orchid"), [Mead's milkweed](/wiki/Mead%27s_milkweed "Mead's milkweed"), [prairie bush clover](/wiki/Prairie_bush_clover "Prairie bush clover"), and [northern wild monkshood](/wiki/Northern_wild_monkshood "Northern wild monkshood").[[83]](#cite_note-85)

The explosion in the number of [high-density livestock facilities](/wiki/Concentrated_animal_feeding_operation "Concentrated animal feeding operation") in Iowa has led to increased rural water contamination and a decline in air quality.[[84]](#cite_note-ISU_1-86)

Other factors negatively affecting Iowa's environment include the extensive use of older [coal-fired power plants](/wiki/Coal-fired_power_plants "Coal-fired power plants"),[[85]](#cite_note-gazette_coal-87) fertilizer and pesticide runoff from crop production,[[86]](#cite_note-IPT_runoff-88) and diminishment of the [Jordan Aquifer](/wiki/Jordan_Aquifer "Jordan Aquifer").[[87]](#cite_note-89)

The [2020–2023 North American drought](/wiki/2020%E2%80%932023_North_American_drought "2020–2023 North American drought") has affected Iowa particularly: As of January 2024, Iowa was in its 187th consecutive week of at least moderate [drought](/wiki/Drought "Drought"), the longest stretch since the 1950s. 96% of areas are affected by drought.[[88]](#cite_note-90)

### Climate

Main articles: [Climate of Iowa](/wiki/Climate_of_Iowa "Climate of Iowa") and [Climate change in Iowa](/wiki/Climate_change_in_Iowa "Climate change in Iowa")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/K%C3%B6ppen_Climate_Types_Iowa.png/250px-K%C3%B6ppen_Climate_Types_Iowa.png)](/wiki/File:K%C3%B6ppen_Climate_Types_Iowa.png)

[Köppen climate types](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") of Iowa, using 1991–2020 [climate normals](/wiki/Climatological_normal "Climatological normal")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Iowa_rainfall.jpg/250px-Iowa_rainfall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Iowa_rainfall.jpg)

Iowa annual rainfall, in inches; as of 2009

Iowa has a [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") throughout the state ([Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") *Dfa*) with extremes of both heat and cold. The average annual temperature at Des Moines is 50 °F (10 °C); for some locations in the north, such as Mason City, the figure is about 45 °F (7 °C), while [Keokuk](/wiki/Keokuk,_Iowa "Keokuk, Iowa"), on the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River"), averages 52 °F (11 °C).[[89]](#cite_note-91) Snowfall is common, with [Des Moines](/wiki/Des_Moines "Des Moines") getting about 26 days of snowfall a year, and other places, such as [Shenandoah](/wiki/Shenandoah,_Iowa "Shenandoah, Iowa"), getting about 11 days of snowfall in a year.[[90]](#cite_note-92)

Spring ushers in the beginning of the [severe weather](/wiki/Severe_weather "Severe weather") season. As of 2008, Iowa averaged about 50 days of [thunderstorm](/wiki/Thunderstorm "Thunderstorm") activity per year.[[91]](#cite_note-Thunderstorm_Hazard-93) As of 2015, the 30-year annual average of tornadoes in Iowa was 47.[[92]](#cite_note-Annual_Average_Number_of_Tornadoes-94) In [2008](/wiki/Late-May_2008_tornado_outbreak_sequence "Late-May 2008 tornado outbreak sequence"), twelve people were killed by tornadoes in Iowa, making it the deadliest year since [1968](/wiki/May_1968_tornado_outbreak "May 1968 tornado outbreak") and also the second most tornadoes in a year with 105, matching the total from 2001.[[93]](#cite_note-95)

Iowa summers are known for heat and humidity, with daytime temperatures sometimes near 90 °F (32 °C) and occasionally exceeding 100 °F (38 °C). Average winters in the state have been known to drop well below freezing, even dropping below −18 °F (−28 °C). As of 2018, Iowa's all-time hottest temperature of 118 °F (48 °C) was recorded at Keokuk on July 20, 1934, during a nationwide heat wave;[[94]](#cite_note-96) as of 2014, the all-time lowest temperature of −47 °F (−44 °C) was recorded in [Washta](/wiki/Washta "Washta") on January 12, 1912.[[95]](#cite_note-97)

#### Precipitation

Iowa has had a relatively smooth gradient of varying [precipitation](/wiki/Precipitation_(meteorology) "Precipitation (meteorology)") across the state; from 1961 to 1990, areas in the southeast of the state received an average of over 38 inches (97 cm) of rain annually, and the northwest of the state receiving less than 28 inches (71 cm).[[102]](#cite_note-104) The pattern of precipitation across Iowa is seasonal with more rain falling in the summer months. Virtually statewide, the driest month is January or February, while the wettest month is June due to frequent showers and thunderstorms, some of which produce hail, damaging winds or tornadoes. In Des Moines, roughly in the center of the state, over two-thirds of the 34.72 inches (88.2 cm) of rain falls from April through September, and about half the average annual precipitation falls from May through August peaking in June.[[103]](#cite_note-105)

### Settlements

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/f/f5/Iowa_pop_00_to_09.jpg)](/wiki/File:Iowa_pop_00_to_09.jpg)

Percent population changes by counties in Iowa, 2000–2009; dark green counties have gains of more than 5%[[104]](#cite_note-106)

See also: [List of cities in Iowa](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Iowa "List of cities in Iowa")

Iowa's population is more urban than rural, with 61 percent living in urban areas in 2000, a trend that began in the early 20th century.[[62]](#cite_note-data.iowadatacenter.org-63) Urban counties in Iowa grew 8.5% from 2000 to 2008, while rural counties declined by 4.2%.[[105]](#cite_note-107) The shift from rural to urban has caused population increases in more urbanized counties such as [Dallas](/wiki/Dallas_County,_Iowa "Dallas County, Iowa"), [Johnson](/wiki/Johnson_County,_Iowa "Johnson County, Iowa"), [Linn](/wiki/Linn_County,_Iowa "Linn County, Iowa"), [Polk](/wiki/Polk_County,_Iowa "Polk County, Iowa"), and [Scott](/wiki/Scott_County,_Iowa "Scott County, Iowa"), at the expense of more rural counties.[[106]](#cite_note-Census-2010-108)

Iowa, in common with other Midwestern states (especially [Kansas](/wiki/Kansas "Kansas"), [Nebraska](/wiki/Nebraska "Nebraska"), [North Dakota](/wiki/North_Dakota "North Dakota"), and [South Dakota](/wiki/South_Dakota "South Dakota")), is feeling the brunt of [rural flight](/wiki/Rural_flight "Rural flight"), although the population of Iowa has been increasing since approximately 1990. Some smaller communities, such as [Denison](/wiki/Denison,_Iowa "Denison, Iowa") and [Storm Lake](/wiki/Storm_Lake,_Iowa "Storm Lake, Iowa"), have mitigated this population loss through gains in immigrant laborers.[[107]](#cite_note-109)

Another demographic problem for Iowa is [brain drain](/wiki/Brain_drain "Brain drain"), in which educated young adults leave the state in search of better prospects in higher education or employment. During the 1990s, Iowa had the second highest exodus rate for single, educated young adults, second only to North Dakota.[[108]](#cite_note-110)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Ethnic_Origins_in_Iowa.png/250px-Ethnic_Origins_in_Iowa.png)](/wiki/File:Ethnic_Origins_in_Iowa.png)

Ethnic origins in Iowa

The [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") determined the population of Iowa was 3,190,369 on April 1, 2020, a 4.73% increase since the [2010 United States census](/wiki/2010_United_States_Census "2010 United States Census").[[112]](#cite_note-2020_Census-114)[[113]](#cite_note-115)

Of the residents of Iowa, 70.8% were born in Iowa, 23.6% were born in a different U.S. state, 0.6% were born in Puerto Rico, U.S. Island areas, or born abroad to American parent(s), and 5% were foreign born.[[114]](#cite_note-116)

[Immigration](/wiki/Immigration_to_the_United_States "Immigration to the United States") from outside the United States resulted in a net increase of 29,386 people, while migration within the country produced a net loss of 41,140 people. 6.5% of Iowa's population were reported as under the age of five, 22.6% under 18, and 14.7% were 65 or older. Males made up approximately 49.6% of the population.[[115]](#cite_note-117) The population density of the state is 52.7 people per square mile.[[116]](#cite_note-118) As of the [2010 census](/wiki/2010_United_States_Census "2010 United States Census"), the [center of population](/wiki/Center_of_population "Center of population") of Iowa is in [Marshall County](/wiki/Marshall_County,_Iowa "Marshall County, Iowa"), near [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne,_Iowa "Melbourne, Iowa").[[117]](#cite_note-119) The top countries of origin for Iowa's immigrants in 2018 were [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"), [India](/wiki/India "India"), [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam"), [China](/wiki/China "China") and [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand").[[118]](#cite_note-120)

According to a version of *[Encyclopædia Britannica](/wiki/Encyclop%C3%A6dia_Britannica "Encyclopædia Britannica")* published in 1999, Germans are the largest ethnic group in Iowa. Other major ethnic groups in Iowa include Irish and English. There are also Dutch communities in state. The Dutch can be found in [Pella](/wiki/Pella,_Iowa "Pella, Iowa"), in the centre of the state, and in Orange City, in the northwest. There is a Norwegian community in Decorah in northeast Iowa; and there is Czech and Slovak communities in both Cedar Rapids and Iowa City. Smaller numbers of Greeks and Italians are scattered in Iowa's metropolitan areas. The majority of Hispanics in Iowa are of [Mexican origin](/wiki/Mexican_Americans "Mexican Americans"). [African Americans](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans"), who constitute around 2% of Iowa's population, didn't live in the state in any appreciable numbers until the early 20th century. Many blacks worked in the coal-mining industry of southern Iowa. Others blacks migrated to Waterloo, Davenport, and Des Moines, where the black population remained substantial in the early 21st century.[[119]](#cite_note-121) The African-American population in Des Moines experienced a significant increase with the establishment of the Colored Officers Training Camp at Fort Des Moines in 1917. Following the conclusion of World War I in 1918, numerous African-American families made the decision to remain in Des Moines. This marked the inception of a thriving community that eventually became a residence for numerous African-American leaders.[[120]](#cite_note-122) There is one [federally recognized](/wiki/List_of_federally_recognized_tribes_by_state "List of federally recognized tribes by state") tribe in Iowa, the [Sac and Fox Tribe of the Mississippi in Iowa](/wiki/Sac_and_Fox_Tribe_of_the_Mississippi_in_Iowa "Sac and Fox Tribe of the Mississippi in Iowa"), and in 2020, 14,486 identified as being Native American alone, and 41,472 did in combination with one or more other races.[[121]](#cite_note-123)

As of the 2010 census, the population of Iowa was 3,046,355. The gender makeup of the state was 49.5% male and 50.5% female. 23.9% of the population were under the age of 18; 61.2% were between the ages of 18 and 64; and 14.9% were 65 years of age or older.[[122]](#cite_note-Census2010-124)

According to [HUD](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Housing_and_Urban_Development "United States Department of Housing and Urban Development")'s 2022 [Annual Homeless Assessment Report](/wiki/Annual_Homeless_Assessment_Report_to_Congress "Annual Homeless Assessment Report to Congress"), there were an estimated 2,419 [homeless](/wiki/Homelessness "Homelessness") people in Iowa.[[123]](#cite_note-125)[[124]](#cite_note-126)

### Religion

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Amana_Colonies.JPG/250px-Amana_Colonies.JPG)](/wiki/File:Amana_Colonies.JPG)

[Amana Colonies](/wiki/Amana_Colonies "Amana Colonies") were founded by [German](/wiki/Germans "Germans") [Pietists](/wiki/Pietists "Pietists")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Iowa_cross_on_hill-2.jpg/250px-Iowa_cross_on_hill-2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Iowa_cross_on_hill-2.jpg)

A [Christian cross](/wiki/Christian_cross "Christian cross") on a hill in Iowa

Religious self-identification, per [Public Religion Research Institute](/wiki/Public_Religion_Research_Institute "Public Religion Research Institute")'s 2022 *American Values Survey*[[146]](#cite_note-150)

1. [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism_in_the_United_States "Protestantism in the United States") (48.0%)
2. [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_the_United_States "Catholic Church in the United States") (20.0%)
3. [Unitarian](/wiki/Unitarianism "Unitarianism")/[Universalist](/wiki/Unitarian_Universalism "Unitarian Universalism") (1.00%)
4. [Unaffiliated](/wiki/Irreligion_in_the_United_States "Irreligion in the United States") (29.0%)
5. Other (2.00%)

A 2014 survey by the [Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center") found that 60% of Iowans are [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant"), while 18% are [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic"), and 1% are of non-Christian religions. 21% responded with non-religious, and 1% did not answer.[[147]](#cite_note-pew2014-151)[[148]](#cite_note-152) A survey from the [Association of Religion Data Archives](/wiki/Association_of_Religion_Data_Archives "Association of Religion Data Archives") (ARDA) in 2010 found that the largest Protestant denominations were the [United Methodist Church](/wiki/United_Methodist_Church "United Methodist Church") with 235,190 adherents and the [Evangelical Lutheran Church in America](/wiki/Evangelical_Lutheran_Church_in_America "Evangelical Lutheran Church in America") with 229,557. The largest non-Protestant religion was [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholicism "Catholicism") with 503,080 adherents. The state has a great number of [Calvinist](/wiki/Calvinist "Calvinist") denominations. The [Presbyterian Church (USA)](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_(USA) "Presbyterian Church (USA)") had almost 290 congregations and 51,380 members followed by the [Reformed Church in America](/wiki/Reformed_Church_in_America "Reformed Church in America") with 80 churches and 40,000 members, and the [United Church of Christ](/wiki/United_Church_of_Christ "United Church of Christ") had 180 churches and 39,000 members.[[149]](#cite_note-www.thearda.com-153) According to the 2020 [Public Religion Research Institute](/wiki/Public_Religion_Research_Institute "Public Religion Research Institute")'s study, 26% of the population were irreligious.[[150]](#cite_note-154)

The study *Religious Congregations & Membership: 2000*[[151]](#cite_note-155) found that in the southernmost two tiers of Iowa counties and in other counties in the center of the state, the largest religious group was the [United Methodist Church](/wiki/United_Methodist_Church "United Methodist Church"); in the northeast part of the state, including [Dubuque](/wiki/Dubuque_County,_Iowa "Dubuque County, Iowa") and [Linn](/wiki/Linn_County,_Iowa "Linn County, Iowa") counties (where [Cedar Rapids](/wiki/Cedar_Rapids "Cedar Rapids") is located), the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") was the largest; and in ten counties, including three in the northern tier, the [Evangelical Lutheran Church in America](/wiki/Evangelical_Lutheran_Church_in_America "Evangelical Lutheran Church in America") was the largest. The study also found rapid growth in Evangelical Christian denominations. Dubuque is home to the [Archdiocese of Dubuque](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Dubuque "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Dubuque"), which serves as the [ecclesiastical province](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_province "Ecclesiastical province") for all three other dioceses in the state and for all the Catholics in Iowa.

Historically, religious sects and orders who desired to live apart from the rest of society established themselves in Iowa; for example, the [Amish](/wiki/Amish "Amish") and [Mennonites](/wiki/Mennonites "Mennonites") have communities near [Kalona](/wiki/Kalona,_Iowa "Kalona, Iowa") and in other parts of eastern Iowa such as [Davis County](/wiki/Davis_County,_Iowa "Davis County, Iowa") and [Buchanan County](/wiki/Buchanan_County,_Iowa "Buchanan County, Iowa").[[152]](#cite_note-156) Other religious sects and orders living apart include [Quakers](/wiki/Quakers "Quakers") around [West Branch](/wiki/West_Branch,_Iowa "West Branch, Iowa") and [Le Grand](/wiki/Le_Grand,_Iowa "Le Grand, Iowa"), German [Pietists](/wiki/Pietists "Pietists") who founded the [Amana Colonies](/wiki/Amana_Colonies "Amana Colonies"), followers of [Transcendental Meditation](/wiki/Transcendental_Meditation "Transcendental Meditation") who founded [Maharishi Vedic City](/wiki/Maharishi_Vedic_City,_Iowa "Maharishi Vedic City, Iowa"), and [Order of Cistercians of the Strict Observance](/wiki/Trappist "Trappist") monks and nuns at the [New Melleray](/wiki/New_Melleray_Abbey "New Melleray Abbey") and [Our Lady of the Mississippi](/wiki/Our_Lady_of_the_Mississippi_Abbey "Our Lady of the Mississippi Abbey") Abbeys near [Dubuque](/wiki/Dubuque,_Iowa "Dubuque, Iowa").

By 1878, approximately 1000 [Jews](/wiki/Jews "Jews"), many of whom were immigrants from Poland and Germany, lived in Iowa.[[153]](#cite_note-157)[[154]](#cite_note-158) As of 2016[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Iowa&action=edit), about 6,000 Jews lived in Iowa, with about 3,000 in Des Moines alone.[[155]](#cite_note-Tapper-159)

### Language

English is the most common language in Iowa, being the sole language spoken by 91.1% of the population. Less common languages include sign language and indigenous languages. About 2.5% of the general population use sign language as of 2017, while indigenous languages are spoken by about 0.5% of the population.[[156]](#cite_note-160) [William Labov](/wiki/William_Labov "William Labov") and colleagues, in the monumental *[Atlas of North American English](/wiki/Atlas_of_North_American_English "Atlas of North American English")*,[[157]](#cite_note-161) found the English spoken in Iowa divides into multiple linguistic regions. Natives of northern Iowa—including [Sioux City](/wiki/Sioux_City "Sioux City"), [Fort Dodge](/wiki/Fort_Dodge "Fort Dodge"), and the [Waterloo](/wiki/Waterloo,_Iowa "Waterloo, Iowa") region—tend to speak the dialect linguists call [North Central American English](/wiki/North_Central_American_English "North Central American English"), which is also found in [North](/wiki/North_Dakota "North Dakota") and [South Dakota](/wiki/South_Dakota "South Dakota"), [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota "Minnesota"), [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin"), and [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan"). Natives of central and southern Iowa—including such cities as [Council Bluffs](/wiki/Council_Bluffs,_Iowa "Council Bluffs, Iowa"), [Davenport](/wiki/Davenport,_Iowa "Davenport, Iowa"), Des Moines, and [Iowa City](/wiki/Iowa_City,_Iowa "Iowa City, Iowa")—tend to speak the [North Midland](/wiki/Midland_American_English "Midland American English") dialect also found in eastern Nebraska, central Illinois, and central Indiana.[[158]](#cite_note-162) Natives of East-Central Iowa—including cities such as [Cedar Rapids](/wiki/Cedar_Rapids "Cedar Rapids"), [Dubuque](/wiki/Dubuque,_Iowa "Dubuque, Iowa"), and [Clinton](/wiki/Clinton,_Iowa "Clinton, Iowa") tend to speak with the [Northern Cities Vowel Shift](/wiki/Northern_Cities_Vowel_Shift "Northern Cities Vowel Shift"), a dialect that extends from this area and east across the [Great Lakes Region](/wiki/Great_Lakes_Region "Great Lakes Region").[[159]](#cite_note-163)

After English, Spanish is the second-most-common language spoken in Iowa, with 120,000 people in Iowa of Hispanic or Latino origin and 47,000 people born in Latin America.[[160]](#cite_note-164) The third-most-common language is German, spoken by 17,000 people in Iowa; two notable German dialects used in Iowa include [Amana German](/wiki/Amana_German "Amana German") spoken around the [Amana Colonies](/wiki/Amana_Colonies "Amana Colonies"), and [Pennsylvania German](/wiki/Pennsylvania_German_language "Pennsylvania German language"), spoken among the [Amish](/wiki/Amish "Amish") in Iowa. The [Babel Proclamation](/wiki/Babel_Proclamation "Babel Proclamation") of 1918 banned the speaking of German in public. Around [Pella](/wiki/Pella,_Iowa "Pella, Iowa"), residents of Dutch descent once spoke the [Pella Dutch dialect](/wiki/Pella_Dutch_dialect "Pella Dutch dialect").

## Culture

### Attractions

Iowa hosts [RAGBRAI](/wiki/RAGBRAI "RAGBRAI"), the Register's Annual Great Bicycle Ride Across Iowa, which is a bike across the state river-to-river that attracts thousands of bicyclists and support personnel. It has crossed the state on various routes each year since 1973. Iowa is home to more than 70 wineries,[[161]](#cite_note-165) and hosts five regional wine tasting trails.[[162]](#cite_note-166) Many Iowa communities hold farmers' markets during warmer months; these are typically weekly events, but larger cities can host multiple markets.[[163]](#cite_note-167)

#### Central Iowa

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/RG_Christina_Reiman_Butterfly_Wing.jpg/250px-RG_Christina_Reiman_Butterfly_Wing.jpg)](/wiki/File:RG_Christina_Reiman_Butterfly_Wing.jpg)

The Christina Reiman Butterfly Wing at [Iowa State University](/wiki/Iowa_State_University "Iowa State University"), Ames

Des Moines is the largest city and metropolitan area[[e]](#cite_note-MSA-168) in Iowa and the state's political and economic center. It is home to the [Iowa State Capitol](/wiki/Iowa_State_Capitol "Iowa State Capitol"), the [State Historical Society of Iowa](/wiki/State_Historical_Society_of_Iowa "State Historical Society of Iowa") Museum, [Drake University](/wiki/Drake_University "Drake University"), [Des Moines Art Center](/wiki/Des_Moines_Art_Center "Des Moines Art Center"), [Greater Des Moines Botanical Garden](/wiki/Greater_Des_Moines_Botanical_Garden "Greater Des Moines Botanical Garden"), [Principal Riverwalk](/wiki/Principal_Riverwalk "Principal Riverwalk"), the [Iowa State Fair](/wiki/Iowa_State_Fair "Iowa State Fair"), [Terrace Hill](/wiki/Terrace_Hill "Terrace Hill"), and the [World Food Prize](/wiki/World_Food_Prize "World Food Prize"). Nearby attractions include [Adventureland](/wiki/Adventureland_(Iowa) "Adventureland (Iowa)") and [Prairie Meadows Racetrack](/wiki/Prairie_Meadows_Racetrack "Prairie Meadows Racetrack") Casino in [Altoona](/wiki/Altoona,_Iowa "Altoona, Iowa"), [Living History Farms](/wiki/Living_History_Farms "Living History Farms") in [Urbandale](/wiki/Urbandale,_Iowa "Urbandale, Iowa"), Trainland USA in [Colfax](/wiki/Colfax,_Iowa "Colfax, Iowa"), the [National Balloon Classic](/wiki/National_Balloon_Classic "National Balloon Classic") and [National Balloon Museum](/wiki/National_Balloon_Museum "National Balloon Museum") in [Indianola](/wiki/Indianola,_Iowa "Indianola, Iowa"), and the [Iowa Speedway](/wiki/Iowa_Speedway "Iowa Speedway") and Valle Drive-In in [Newton](/wiki/Newton,_Iowa "Newton, Iowa").

[Ames](/wiki/Ames,_Iowa "Ames, Iowa") is the home of [Iowa State University](/wiki/Iowa_State_University "Iowa State University"), the [Iowa State Center](/wiki/Iowa_State_Center "Iowa State Center"), and [Reiman Gardens](/wiki/Reiman_Gardens "Reiman Gardens").

[Boone](/wiki/Boone,_Iowa "Boone, Iowa") hosts the biennial [Farm Progress Show](/wiki/Farm_Progress_Show "Farm Progress Show") and is home to the [Mamie Doud Eisenhower](/wiki/Mamie_Eisenhower "Mamie Eisenhower") museum, the [Boone and Scenic Valley Railroad](/wiki/Boone_and_Scenic_Valley_Railroad "Boone and Scenic Valley Railroad"), and [Ledges State Park](/wiki/Ledges_State_Park "Ledges State Park").

The [Meskwaki Settlement](/wiki/Meskwaki_Settlement "Meskwaki Settlement") west of [Tama](/wiki/Tama,_Iowa "Tama, Iowa") is the only Native American settlement in Iowa and is host to a large annual [Pow-wow](/wiki/Pow-wow "Pow-wow").

[Madison County](/wiki/Madison_County,_Iowa "Madison County, Iowa") is known for its covered bridges. Also in Madison County is the [John Wayne](/wiki/John_Wayne "John Wayne") Birthplace Museum is in [Winterset](/wiki/Winterset,_Iowa "Winterset, Iowa").

Other communities with vibrant historic downtown areas include [Newton](/wiki/Newton,_Iowa "Newton, Iowa"), [Indianola](/wiki/Indianola,_Iowa "Indianola, Iowa"), [Pella](/wiki/Pella,_Iowa "Pella, Iowa"), [Knoxville](/wiki/Knoxville,_Iowa "Knoxville, Iowa"), [Marshalltown](/wiki/Marshalltown,_Iowa "Marshalltown, Iowa"), [Perry](/wiki/Perry,_Iowa "Perry, Iowa"), and [Story City](/wiki/Story_City,_Iowa "Story City, Iowa").

[Brucemore](/wiki/Brucemore "Brucemore"), Cedar Rapids

[Iowa City](/wiki/Iowa_City "Iowa City") is home to the [University of Iowa](/wiki/University_of_Iowa "University of Iowa"), which includes the [Iowa Writers' Workshop](/wiki/Iowa_Writers%27_Workshop "Iowa Writers' Workshop"), and the [Old Capitol building](/wiki/Iowa_Old_Capitol_Building "Iowa Old Capitol Building"). Because of the extraordinary history in the teaching and sponsoring of creative writing that emanated from the [Iowa Writers' Workshop](/wiki/Iowa_Writers%27_Workshop "Iowa Writers' Workshop") and related programs, Iowa City was the first American city designated by the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") as a "[City of Literature](/wiki/City_of_Literature "City of Literature")" in the [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") Creative Cities Network.[[164]](#cite_note-169)

The [Herbert Hoover National Historic Site](/wiki/Herbert_Hoover_National_Historic_Site "Herbert Hoover National Historic Site") and [Herbert Hoover Presidential Library and Museum](/wiki/Herbert_Hoover_Presidential_Library_and_Museum "Herbert Hoover Presidential Library and Museum") are in [West Branch](/wiki/West_Branch,_Iowa "West Branch, Iowa").

The [Amana Colonies](/wiki/Amana_Colonies,_Iowa "Amana Colonies, Iowa") are a group of settlements of [German](/wiki/Germans "Germans") [Pietists](/wiki/Pietists "Pietists") comprising seven villages listed as [National Historic Landmarks](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark").

The [Cedar Rapids Museum of Art](/wiki/Cedar_Rapids_Museum_of_Art "Cedar Rapids Museum of Art") has collections of paintings by [Grant Wood](/wiki/Grant_Wood "Grant Wood") and [Marvin Cone](/wiki/Marvin_Cone "Marvin Cone"). [Cedar Rapids](/wiki/Cedar_Rapids,_Iowa "Cedar Rapids, Iowa") is also home to the [National Czech & Slovak Museum & Library](/wiki/National_Czech_%26_Slovak_Museum_%26_Library "National Czech & Slovak Museum & Library") and Iowa's only [National Trust for Historic Preservation](/wiki/National_Trust_for_Historic_Preservation "National Trust for Historic Preservation") Site, [Brucemore](/wiki/Brucemore "Brucemore") mansion.

[Davenport](/wiki/Davenport,_Iowa "Davenport, Iowa") boasts the [Figge Art Museum](/wiki/Figge_Art_Museum "Figge Art Museum"), [River Music Experience](/wiki/River_Music_Experience "River Music Experience"), [Putnam Museum](/wiki/Putnam_Museum "Putnam Museum"), [Davenport Skybridge](/wiki/Davenport_Skybridge "Davenport Skybridge"), [Quad City Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Quad_City_Symphony_Orchestra "Quad City Symphony Orchestra"), [Ballet Quad Cities](/wiki/Ballet_Quad_Cities "Ballet Quad Cities"), and plays host to the annual [Bix Beiderbecke Memorial Jazz Festival](/wiki/Bix_Beiderbecke_Memorial_Jazz_Festival "Bix Beiderbecke Memorial Jazz Festival"), and the [Quad City Air Show](/wiki/Quad_City_Air_Show "Quad City Air Show"), which is the largest airshow in the state.

Other communities with vibrant historic downtown areas include [West Liberty](/wiki/West_Liberty,_Iowa "West Liberty, Iowa"), [Fairfield](/wiki/Fairfield,_Iowa "Fairfield, Iowa"), [Burlington](/wiki/Burlington,_Iowa "Burlington, Iowa"), [Mount Pleasant](/wiki/Mount_Pleasant,_Iowa "Mount Pleasant, Iowa"), [Fort Madison](/wiki/Fort_Madison,_Iowa "Fort Madison, Iowa"), [LeClaire](/wiki/LeClaire,_Iowa "LeClaire, Iowa"), [Mount Vernon](/wiki/Mount_Vernon,_Iowa "Mount Vernon, Iowa"), [Ottumwa](/wiki/Ottumwa,_Iowa "Ottumwa, Iowa"), [Washington](/wiki/Washington,_Iowa "Washington, Iowa"), and [Wilton](/wiki/Wilton,_Iowa "Wilton, Iowa").

Along Interstate 80 near [Walcott](/wiki/Walcott,_Iowa "Walcott, Iowa") lies the world's largest truck stop, [Iowa 80](/wiki/Iowa_80 "Iowa 80").

#### Western Iowa

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Loess_hills.jpg/250px-Loess_hills.jpg)](/wiki/File:Loess_hills.jpg)

Loess Hills east of [Mondamin](/wiki/Mondamin,_Iowa "Mondamin, Iowa")

Some of the most dramatic scenery in Iowa is found in the unique [Loess Hills](/wiki/Loess_Hills "Loess Hills") which are found along Iowa's western border.

[Sioux City](/wiki/Sioux_City,_Iowa "Sioux City, Iowa") is the largest city in western Iowa and is found on the convergence of the [Missouri](/wiki/Missouri_River "Missouri River"), [Floyd](/wiki/Floyd_River "Floyd River"), and [Big Sioux](/wiki/Big_Sioux_River "Big Sioux River") Rivers. The [Sioux City Metropolitan Area](/wiki/Sioux_City_metropolitan_area "Sioux City metropolitan area") encompasses areas in three states: Iowa, [Nebraska](/wiki/Nebraska "Nebraska"), and [South Dakota](/wiki/South_Dakota "South Dakota"). Sioux City boasts a revitalized downtown and includes attractions such as the [Hard Rock Hotel and Casino](/wiki/Hard_Rock_Hotel_%26_Casino_Sioux_City "Hard Rock Hotel & Casino Sioux City"), [Sergeant Floyd Monument](/wiki/Sergeant_Floyd_Monument "Sergeant Floyd Monument"), [Sergeant Floyd](/wiki/Sergeant_Floyd "Sergeant Floyd") River Museum, the [Tyson Events Center](/wiki/Tyson_Events_Center "Tyson Events Center"), [Southern Hills Mall](/wiki/Southern_Hills_Mall "Southern Hills Mall"), the [Orpheum Theater](/wiki/Orpheum_Theater_(Sioux_City) "Orpheum Theater (Sioux City)"), and more. The historic downtown area is also filled with multiple restaurants, bars, and other entertainment venues. Sioux City is home to two higher education institutions, [Morningside College](/wiki/Morningside_University "Morningside University") and [Briar Cliff University](/wiki/Briar_Cliff_University "Briar Cliff University"). [Le Mars](/wiki/Le_Mars,_Iowa "Le Mars, Iowa") is in the northeastern part of the [Sioux City Metropolitan Area](/wiki/Sioux_City_metropolitan_area "Sioux City metropolitan area") and is the self-proclaimed "Ice Cream Capital of the World". Le Mars is home to [Wells Enterprises](/wiki/Wells_Enterprises "Wells Enterprises"), one of the largest ice cream manufacturers in the world. Attractions in Le Mars include the Wells Visitor Center and Ice Cream Parlor, Archie's Wayside (steak house), Bob's Drive Inn, [Tonsfeldt Round Barn](/wiki/Tonsfeldt_Round_Barn "Tonsfeldt Round Barn"), Plymouth County Fairgrounds, Plymouth County Museum, and [Plymouth County Courthouse](/wiki/Plymouth_County_Courthouse_(Iowa) "Plymouth County Courthouse (Iowa)"). Le Mars hosts multiple ice cream-themed community events each year.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/1/10/Grotto_Arcade.jpg/250px-Grotto_Arcade.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grotto_Arcade.jpg)

View of [Grotto of the Redemption](/wiki/Grotto_of_the_Redemption "Grotto of the Redemption")'s Lower Arcade: Small Stations of the Cross, [West Bend](/wiki/West_Bend,_Iowa "West Bend, Iowa")

[Council Bluffs](/wiki/Council_Bluffs,_Iowa "Council Bluffs, Iowa"), part of the [Omaha, Nebraska Metropolitan Area](/wiki/Omaha_Metro "Omaha Metro") and a hub of southwest Iowa sits at the base of the Loess Hills National Scenic Byway. With three casino resorts, the city also includes such cultural attractions as the Western Hills Trails Center, [Union Pacific Railroad](/wiki/Union_Pacific_Railroad "Union Pacific Railroad") Museum, the [Grenville M. Dodge House](/wiki/Grenville_M._Dodge_House "Grenville M. Dodge House"), the [Ruth Anne Dodge Memorial](/wiki/Ruth_Anne_Dodge_Memorial "Ruth Anne Dodge Memorial"), and the [Lewis and Clark](/wiki/Lewis_and_Clark "Lewis and Clark") Monument, with clear views of the [Downtown Omaha](/wiki/Downtown_Omaha "Downtown Omaha") skyline found throughout the city. The Sanford Museum and Planetarium in [Cherokee](/wiki/Cherokee,_Iowa "Cherokee, Iowa"), the [Grotto of the Redemption](/wiki/Grotto_of_the_Redemption "Grotto of the Redemption") in [West Bend](/wiki/West_Bend,_Iowa "West Bend, Iowa"), the [Museum of Danish America](/wiki/Museum_of_Danish_America "Museum of Danish America") in [Elk Horn](/wiki/Elk_Horn,_Iowa "Elk Horn, Iowa"), and the Fort Museum and Frontier Village in [Fort Dodge](/wiki/Fort_Dodge,_Iowa "Fort Dodge, Iowa") are other regional destinations.

The [Iowa Great Lakes](/wiki/Iowa_Great_Lakes "Iowa Great Lakes") is made up of multiple small towns, such as [Spirit Lake](/wiki/Spirit_Lake,_Iowa "Spirit Lake, Iowa"), [Arnolds Park](/wiki/Arnolds_Park,_Iowa "Arnolds Park, Iowa"), [Milford](/wiki/Milford,_Iowa "Milford, Iowa"), and [Okoboji](/wiki/Okoboji "Okoboji"). Multiple resorts and other tourist attractions are found in and around these towns surrounding the popular lakes. Arnolds Park, one of the oldest amusement parks in the country, is located on Lake Okoboji in [Arnolds Park](/wiki/Arnolds_Park,_Iowa "Arnolds Park, Iowa").

Every year in early May, the city of [Orange City](/wiki/Orange_City,_Iowa "Orange City, Iowa") holds the annual Tulip Festival, a celebration of the strong Dutch heritage in the region.[[165]](#cite_note-170)

Northwest Iowa is home to some of the largest concentrations of [wind turbine](/wiki/Wind_turbine "Wind turbine") farms in the world. Other western communities with vibrant historic downtown areas include [Storm Lake](/wiki/Storm_Lake,_Iowa "Storm Lake, Iowa"), [Spencer](/wiki/Spencer,_Iowa "Spencer, Iowa"), [Glenwood](/wiki/Glenwood,_Iowa "Glenwood, Iowa"), [Carroll](/wiki/Carroll,_Iowa "Carroll, Iowa"), [Harlan](/wiki/Harlan,_Iowa "Harlan, Iowa"), [Atlantic](/wiki/Atlantic,_Iowa "Atlantic, Iowa"), [Red Oak](/wiki/Red_Oak,_Iowa "Red Oak, Iowa"), [Denison](/wiki/Denison,_Iowa "Denison, Iowa"), [Creston](/wiki/Creston,_Iowa "Creston, Iowa"), [Mount Ayr](/wiki/Mount_Ayr,_Iowa "Mount Ayr, Iowa"), [Sac City](/wiki/Sac_City,_Iowa "Sac City, Iowa"), and [Walnut](/wiki/Walnut,_Iowa "Walnut, Iowa").

#### Northeast and Northern Iowa

"Northern Iowa" redirects here. For the University of Northern Iowa, see [University of Northern Iowa](/wiki/University_of_Northern_Iowa "University of Northern Iowa").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Fort_Atkinson_Iowa.jpg/250px-Fort_Atkinson_Iowa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fort_Atkinson_Iowa.jpg)

Ruins of historic [Fort Atkinson](/wiki/Fort_Atkinson_State_Preserve "Fort Atkinson State Preserve")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/FloatingSauna.jpg/250px-FloatingSauna.jpg)](/wiki/File:FloatingSauna.jpg)

Wood-heated floating [sauna](/wiki/Sauna "Sauna") on the farm pond

The [Driftless Area](/wiki/Driftless_Area "Driftless Area") of northeast Iowa has many steep hills and deep valleys, checkered with forest and terraced fields. [Effigy Mounds National Monument](/wiki/Effigy_Mounds_National_Monument "Effigy Mounds National Monument") in [Allamakee](/wiki/Allamakee_County,_Iowa "Allamakee County, Iowa") and [Clayton Counties](/wiki/Clayton_County,_Iowa "Clayton County, Iowa") has the largest assemblage of animal-shaped prehistoric mounds in the world.

[Waterloo](/wiki/Waterloo,_Iowa "Waterloo, Iowa") is home of the [Grout Museum](/wiki/Grout_Museum "Grout Museum") and [Lost Island Theme Park](/wiki/Lost_Island_Theme_Park "Lost Island Theme Park") and is headquarters of the [Silos & Smokestacks National Heritage Area](/wiki/Silos_%26_Smokestacks_National_Heritage_Area "Silos & Smokestacks National Heritage Area"). [Cedar Falls](/wiki/Cedar_Falls,_Iowa "Cedar Falls, Iowa") is home of the [University of Northern Iowa](/wiki/University_of_Northern_Iowa "University of Northern Iowa").

[Dubuque](/wiki/Dubuque,_Iowa "Dubuque, Iowa") is a regional tourist destination with attractions such as the [National Mississippi River Museum and Aquarium](/wiki/National_Mississippi_River_Museum_and_Aquarium "National Mississippi River Museum and Aquarium") and the [Port of Dubuque](/wiki/Port_of_Dubuque "Port of Dubuque").

[Dyersville](/wiki/Dyersville,_Iowa "Dyersville, Iowa") is home to the famed *[Field of Dreams](/wiki/Field_of_Dreams_(Dubuque_County,_Iowa) "Field of Dreams (Dubuque County, Iowa)")* baseball diamond. [Maquoketa Caves State Park](/wiki/Maquoketa_Caves_State_Park "Maquoketa Caves State Park"), near [Maquoketa](/wiki/Maquoketa,_Iowa "Maquoketa, Iowa"), contains more caves than any other state park.

[Fort Atkinson State Preserve](/wiki/Fort_Atkinson_State_Preserve "Fort Atkinson State Preserve") in [Fort Atkinson](/wiki/Fort_Atkinson,_Iowa "Fort Atkinson, Iowa") has the remains of an original 1840s [Dragoon](/wiki/Dragoon "Dragoon") fortification.

[Fort Dodge](/wiki/Fort_Dodge "Fort Dodge") is home of The Fort historical museum and the Blanden Art Museum, and host Frontiers Days which celebrate the town history.

Other communities with vibrant historic downtown areas include [Decorah](/wiki/Decorah,_Iowa "Decorah, Iowa"), [McGregor](/wiki/McGregor,_Iowa "McGregor, Iowa"), [Mason City](/wiki/Mason_City,_Iowa "Mason City, Iowa"), [Elkader](/wiki/Elkader,_Iowa "Elkader, Iowa"), [Bellevue](/wiki/Bellevue,_Iowa "Bellevue, Iowa"), [Guttenberg](/wiki/Guttenberg,_Iowa "Guttenberg, Iowa"), [Algona](/wiki/Algona,_Iowa "Algona, Iowa"), [Spillville](/wiki/Spillville,_Iowa "Spillville, Iowa"), [Charles City](/wiki/Charles_City,_Iowa "Charles City, Iowa"), and [Independence](/wiki/Independence,_Iowa "Independence, Iowa").

### Arts

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Schaeffer_Hall%2C_College_of_Liberal_Arts%2C_University_of_Iowa%2C_Iowa_City_%2836501051952%29.jpg/250px-Schaeffer_Hall%2C_College_of_Liberal_Arts%2C_University_of_Iowa%2C_Iowa_City_%2836501051952%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Schaeffer_Hall,_College_of_Liberal_Arts,_University_of_Iowa,_Iowa_City_(36501051952).jpg)

Schaeffer Hall (University of Iowa, Iowa City)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Future_Birthplace_of_Captain_James_T_Kirk.jpg/250px-Future_Birthplace_of_Captain_James_T_Kirk.jpg)](/wiki/File:Future_Birthplace_of_Captain_James_T_Kirk.jpg)

[Riverside](/wiki/Riverside,_Iowa "Riverside, Iowa")'s "favorite son"

The [Clint Eastwood](/wiki/Clint_Eastwood "Clint Eastwood") movie [*The Bridges of Madison County*](/wiki/The_Bridges_of_Madison_County_(film) "The Bridges of Madison County (film)"), based on the popular [novel of the same name](/wiki/The_Bridges_of_Madison_County "The Bridges of Madison County"), took place and was filmed in [Madison County](/wiki/Madison_County,_Iowa "Madison County, Iowa").[[166]](#cite_note-171) *[What's Eating Gilbert Grape](/wiki/What%27s_Eating_Gilbert_Grape "What's Eating Gilbert Grape")*, based on the [Peter Hedges](/wiki/Peter_Hedges "Peter Hedges") novel of the same name, is set in the fictional Iowa town of Endora. Hedges was born in [West Des Moines](/wiki/West_Des_Moines "West Des Moines").[[167]](#cite_note-172)

Des Moines is home to members of the [heavy metal](/wiki/Heavy_metal_music "Heavy metal music") band [Slipknot](/wiki/Slipknot_(band) "Slipknot (band)"). The state is mentioned in the band's songs, and the album [*Iowa*](/wiki/Iowa_(album) "Iowa (album)") is named after the state.[[168]](#cite_note-173)

### Sports

See also: [Sports teams from Iowa](/wiki/Sports_teams_from_Iowa "Sports teams from Iowa")

The state has four major college teams playing in [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") for all sports. In football, [Iowa State University](/wiki/Iowa_State_University "Iowa State University") and the [University of Iowa](/wiki/University_of_Iowa "University of Iowa") compete in the [Football Bowl Subdivision](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Bowl_Subdivision "NCAA Division I Football Bowl Subdivision") (FBS), whereas the [University of Northern Iowa](/wiki/University_of_Northern_Iowa "University of Northern Iowa") and [Drake University](/wiki/Drake_University "Drake University") compete in the [Football Championship Subdivision](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Championship_Subdivision "NCAA Division I Football Championship Subdivision") (FCS), with the added distinction that UNI awards football scholarships and Drake does not. Although Iowa has no professional major league sports teams, Iowa has minor league sports teams in baseball, basketball, hockey, and other sports.

The following table shows the Iowa sports teams with average attendance over 8,000. All the following teams are NCAA Division I football, basketball, or wrestling teams:[[169]](#cite_note-174)[[170]](#cite_note-175)[[171]](#cite_note-176)[[172]](#cite_note-177)
