#### College sports

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Hilton_Coliseum_Inside_View.jpg/250px-Hilton_Coliseum_Inside_View.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hilton_Coliseum_Inside_View.jpg)

[Hilton Coliseum](/wiki/Hilton_Coliseum "Hilton Coliseum"), Iowa State University

The state has four NCAA Division I college teams. Two have football teams that play in the top level of college football, the [Football Bowl Subdivision](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Bowl_Subdivision "NCAA Division I Football Bowl Subdivision"): the [University of Iowa](/wiki/University_of_Iowa "University of Iowa") [Hawkeyes](/wiki/Iowa_Hawkeyes "Iowa Hawkeyes") play in the [Big Ten Conference](/wiki/Big_Ten_Conference "Big Ten Conference")[[178]](#cite_note-183) and the [Iowa State University](/wiki/Iowa_State_University "Iowa State University") [Cyclones](/wiki/Iowa_State_Cyclones "Iowa State Cyclones") compete in the [Big 12 Conference](/wiki/Big_12_Conference "Big 12 Conference").[[179]](#cite_note-184) The two intrastate rivals compete annually for the [Cy-Hawk Trophy](/wiki/Cy-Hawk_Trophy "Cy-Hawk Trophy") as part of the [Iowa Corn Cy-Hawk Series](/wiki/Iowa_Corn_Cy-Hawk_Series "Iowa Corn Cy-Hawk Series").[[180]](#cite_note-185)

In [men's wrestling](/wiki/Collegiate_wrestling "Collegiate wrestling"), the [Iowa Hawkeyes](/wiki/Iowa_Hawkeyes_wrestling "Iowa Hawkeyes wrestling") and [Iowa State Cyclones](/wiki/Iowa_State_Cyclones_wrestling "Iowa State Cyclones wrestling") have won a combined total of over 30 team [NCAA Division I titles](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Wrestling_Championships "NCAA Division I Wrestling Championships").[[181]](#cite_note-186)[[182]](#cite_note-187) The [Northern Iowa](/wiki/Northern_Iowa_Panthers_wrestling "Northern Iowa Panthers wrestling") and [Cornell College](/wiki/Cornell_College "Cornell College") wrestling teams have also each won one NCAA Division I wrestling team title.[[183]](#cite_note-188)[[184]](#cite_note-189) Cornell College now competes in [NCAA Division III](/wiki/NCAA_Division_III "NCAA Division III"), in which another Iowa school, [Wartburg College](/wiki/Wartburg_Knights_men%27s_wrestling "Wartburg Knights men's wrestling"), has won 16 team titles. In [women's wrestling](/wiki/Freestyle_wrestling "Freestyle wrestling"), which became an official NCAA championship sport in 2025–26, 10 Iowa schools [field NCAA varsity teams](/wiki/List_of_NCAA_women%27s_wrestling_programs "List of NCAA women's wrestling programs")—Iowa (one of only six Division I schools to sponsor the sport), [Division II](/wiki/NCAA_Division_II "NCAA Division II") [Upper Iowa University](/wiki/Upper_Iowa_Peacocks "Upper Iowa Peacocks"), and eight Division III members.

Two other Division I schools play football in the second level of college football, the [Football Championship Subdivision](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Championship_Subdivision "NCAA Division I Football Championship Subdivision"). The [University of Northern Iowa](/wiki/University_of_Northern_Iowa "University of Northern Iowa") [Panthers](/wiki/Northern_Iowa_Panthers "Northern Iowa Panthers") (also known as the UNI Panthers) play in the [Missouri Valley Conference](/wiki/Missouri_Valley_Conference "Missouri Valley Conference")[[185]](#cite_note-190) and [Missouri Valley Football Conference](/wiki/Missouri_Valley_Football_Conference "Missouri Valley Football Conference")[[186]](#cite_note-191) (despite the similar names, the conferences are administratively separate, though established a formal partnership in 2025), whereas the [Drake University](/wiki/Drake_University "Drake University") [Bulldogs](/wiki/Drake_Bulldogs "Drake Bulldogs") play in the Missouri Valley Conference[[187]](#cite_note-192) in most sports and [Pioneer League](/wiki/Pioneer_Football_League "Pioneer Football League") for football.[[188]](#cite_note-193)

#### Baseball

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Modern_Woodmen_Park_2.jpg/250px-Modern_Woodmen_Park_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Modern_Woodmen_Park_2.jpg)

[Modern Woodmen Park](/wiki/Modern_Woodmen_Park "Modern Woodmen Park") is home to the [Quad Cities](/wiki/Quad_Cities_River_Bandits "Quad Cities River Bandits") baseball team

Des Moines is home to the [Iowa Cubs](/wiki/Iowa_Cubs "Iowa Cubs"), a [Triple-A](/wiki/Triple-A_(baseball) "Triple-A (baseball)") [Minor League Baseball](/wiki/Minor_League_Baseball "Minor League Baseball") team of the [International League](/wiki/International_League "International League") and affiliate of the [Chicago Cubs](/wiki/Chicago_Cubs "Chicago Cubs").[[189]](#cite_note-194)[[190]](#cite_note-195) Iowa has two [High-A](/wiki/High-A "High-A") minor league teams in the [Midwest League](/wiki/Midwest_League "Midwest League"): the [Cedar Rapids Kernels](/wiki/Cedar_Rapids_Kernels "Cedar Rapids Kernels") ([Minnesota Twins](/wiki/Minnesota_Twins "Minnesota Twins")) and the [Quad Cities River Bandits](/wiki/Quad_Cities_River_Bandits "Quad Cities River Bandits") ([Kansas City Royals](/wiki/Kansas_City_Royals "Kansas City Royals")) who play in Davenport.[[191]](#cite_note-196) The [Sioux City Explorers](/wiki/Sioux_City_Explorers "Sioux City Explorers") are part of the [American Association of Professional Baseball](/wiki/American_Association_of_Professional_Baseball "American Association of Professional Baseball").[[192]](#cite_note-197)

#### Ice hockey

Des Moines is home to the [Iowa Wild](/wiki/Iowa_Wild "Iowa Wild"), who are affiliated with the [Minnesota Wild](/wiki/Minnesota_Wild "Minnesota Wild") and are members of the [American Hockey League](/wiki/American_Hockey_League "American Hockey League").[[193]](#cite_note-198) [Coralville](/wiki/Coralville,_Iowa "Coralville, Iowa") has an [ECHL](/wiki/ECHL "ECHL") team called the [Iowa Heartlanders](/wiki/Iowa_Heartlanders "Iowa Heartlanders") that started playing in the 2021–22 season. The Heartlanders are also an affiliate of the [Minnesota Wild](/wiki/Minnesota_Wild "Minnesota Wild").[[194]](#cite_note-199)

The [United States Hockey League](/wiki/United_States_Hockey_League "United States Hockey League") has five teams in Iowa: the [Cedar Rapids RoughRiders](/wiki/Cedar_Rapids_RoughRiders "Cedar Rapids RoughRiders"), [Sioux City Musketeers](/wiki/Sioux_City_Musketeers "Sioux City Musketeers"), [Waterloo Black Hawks](/wiki/Waterloo_Black_Hawks "Waterloo Black Hawks"), [Des Moines Buccaneers](/wiki/Des_Moines_Buccaneers "Des Moines Buccaneers"), and the [Dubuque Fighting Saints](/wiki/Dubuque_Fighting_Saints "Dubuque Fighting Saints").[[195]](#cite_note-200) The [North Iowa Bulls](/wiki/North_Iowa_Bulls "North Iowa Bulls") of the [North American Hockey League](/wiki/North_American_Hockey_League "North American Hockey League") (NAHL) and the [Mason City Toros](/wiki/Mason_City_Toros "Mason City Toros") of the [North American 3 Hockey League](/wiki/North_American_3_Hockey_League "North American 3 Hockey League") (NA3HL) both play in Mason City.[[196]](#cite_note-201)[[197]](#cite_note-202)

#### Soccer

* The [Des Moines Menace](/wiki/Des_Moines_Menace "Des Moines Menace") of the [USL League Two](/wiki/USL_League_Two "USL League Two") play their home games at Valley Stadium in West Des Moines, Iowa.
* The Cedar Rapids Inferno Soccer Club of the [Midwest Premier League](/wiki/Midwest_Premier_League "Midwest Premier League") play their home games at Robert W. Plaster Athletic Complex at [Mount Mercy University](/wiki/Mount_Mercy_University "Mount Mercy University")
* The [Iowa Raptors FC](/wiki/Iowa_Raptors_FC "Iowa Raptors FC") of the [United Premier Soccer League](/wiki/United_Premier_Soccer_League "United Premier Soccer League") play their home games at [Prairie High School](/wiki/Prairie_High_School_(Iowa) "Prairie High School (Iowa)") in [Cedar Rapids, Iowa](/wiki/Cedar_Rapids,_Iowa "Cedar Rapids, Iowa")
* Union Dubuque F.C. of the [Midwest Premier League](/wiki/Midwest_Premier_League "Midwest Premier League")

#### Other sports

Iowa has two professional basketball teams. The [Iowa Wolves](/wiki/Iowa_Wolves "Iowa Wolves"), an [NBA G League](/wiki/NBA_G_League "NBA G League") team that plays in Des Moines, is owned and affiliated with the [Minnesota Timberwolves](/wiki/Minnesota_Timberwolves "Minnesota Timberwolves") of the [NBA](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association"). The Sioux City Hornets play in the [American Basketball Association](/wiki/American_Basketball_Association_(2000%E2%80%93present) "American Basketball Association (2000–present)").

Iowa has three professional football teams. The [Sioux City Bandits](/wiki/Sioux_City_Bandits "Sioux City Bandits") play in the [Champions Indoor Football](/wiki/Champions_Indoor_Football "Champions Indoor Football") league. The [Iowa Barnstormers](/wiki/Iowa_Barnstormers "Iowa Barnstormers") play in the [Indoor Football League](/wiki/Indoor_Football_League "Indoor Football League") at [Casey's Center](/wiki/Casey%27s_Center "Casey's Center") in Des Moines. The [Cedar Rapids Titans](/wiki/Cedar_Rapids_Titans "Cedar Rapids Titans") play in the [Indoor Football League](/wiki/Indoor_Football_League "Indoor Football League") at the [U.S. Cellular Center](/wiki/U.S._Cellular_Center_(Cedar_Rapids,_Iowa) "U.S. Cellular Center (Cedar Rapids, Iowa)").

The [Iowa Speedway](/wiki/Iowa_Speedway "Iowa Speedway") oval track in [Newton](/wiki/Newton,_Iowa "Newton, Iowa") has hosted auto racing championships such as the [IndyCar Series](/wiki/IndyCar_Series "IndyCar Series"), [NASCAR Xfinity Series](/wiki/NASCAR_Xfinity_Series "NASCAR Xfinity Series") and [NASCAR Truck Series](/wiki/NASCAR_Truck_Series "NASCAR Truck Series") since 2006. Also, the [Knoxville Raceway](/wiki/Knoxville_Raceway "Knoxville Raceway") dirt track hosts the [Knoxville Nationals](/wiki/Knoxville_Nationals "Knoxville Nationals"), one of the classic [sprint car racing](/wiki/Sprint_car_racing "Sprint car racing") events.

The [John Deere Classic](/wiki/John_Deere_Classic "John Deere Classic") is a [PGA Tour](/wiki/PGA_Tour "PGA Tour") golf event held in the Quad Cities since 1971. The [Principal Charity Classic](/wiki/Principal_Charity_Classic "Principal Charity Classic") is a [Champions Tour](/wiki/Champions_Tour "Champions Tour") event since 2001. The [Des Moines Golf and Country Club](/wiki/Des_Moines_Golf_and_Country_Club "Des Moines Golf and Country Club") hosted the 1999 [U.S. Senior Open](/wiki/U.S._Senior_Open "U.S. Senior Open") and the 2017 [Solheim Cup](/wiki/Solheim_Cup "Solheim Cup").

## Economy

See also: [Iowa locations by per capita income](/wiki/Iowa_locations_by_per_capita_income "Iowa locations by per capita income")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Iowa_products_2009.jpg/500px-Iowa_products_2009.jpg)](/wiki/File:Iowa_products_2009.jpg)

Iowa [gross state products](/wiki/Gross_state_product "Gross state product") by industry, 2009[[198]](#cite_note-203)

In 2016, the total employment of the state's population was 1,354,487, and the total number of employer establishments was 81,563.[[199]](#cite_note-204) In 2025, [small businesses](/wiki/Small_business "Small business") made up 99.3% of all businesses in the state, employing 46.0% of Iowa's work force.[[200]](#cite_note-205)

CNBC's list of "Top States for Business in 2010" has recognized Iowa as the sixth best state in the nation. Scored in 10 individual categories, Iowa was ranked first when it came to the "Cost of Doing Business"; this includes all taxes, utility costs, and other costs associated with doing business. Iowa was also ranked 10th in "Economy", 12th in "Business Friendliness", 16th in "Education", 17th in both "Cost of Living" and "Quality of Life", 20th in "Workforce", 29th in "Technology and Innovation", 32nd in "Transportation" and the lowest ranking was 36th in "Access to Capital".[[201]](#cite_note-206)

While Iowa is often viewed as a farming state, agriculture is a relatively small portion of the state's diversified economy, with manufacturing, biotechnology, finance and insurance services, and government services contributing substantially to Iowa's economy.[[60]](#cite_note-iowalifechanging.com-61) This economic diversity has helped Iowa weather the [late 2000s recession](/wiki/Late-2000s_recession "Late-2000s recession") better than most states, with unemployment substantially lower than the rest of the nation.[[202]](#cite_note-207)[[203]](#cite_note-208)

If the economy is measured by gross domestic product, in 2005 Iowa's GDP was about $124 billion.[[204]](#cite_note-209) If measured by gross state product, for 2005 it was $113.5 billion.[[205]](#cite_note-Facts-210) Its per capita income for 2006 was $23,340.[[205]](#cite_note-Facts-210) On July 2, 2009, [Standard & Poor's](/wiki/Standard_%26_Poor%27s "Standard & Poor's") rated Iowa's credit as AAA (the highest of its credit ratings, held by only 11 U.S. state governments).[[206]](#cite_note-211)

As of May 2025, the state's unemployment rate was 3.6%.[[207]](#cite_note-212)

### Manufacturing

[Manufacturing](/wiki/Manufacturing "Manufacturing") is the largest sector of Iowa's economy, with $20.8 billion (21%) of Iowa's 2003 gross state product. Major manufacturing sectors include [food processing](/wiki/Food_processing "Food processing"), heavy machinery, and [agricultural chemicals](/wiki/Agrochemical "Agrochemical"). Sixteen percent of Iowa's workforce is dedicated to manufacturing.[[60]](#cite_note-iowalifechanging.com-61) Food processing is the largest component of manufacturing. Besides processed food, industrial outputs include machinery, electric equipment, chemical products, publishing, and primary metals. Companies with direct or indirect processing facilities in Iowa include [ConAgra Foods](/wiki/ConAgra_Foods "ConAgra Foods"), [Wells Blue Bunny](/wiki/Wells_Dairy "Wells Dairy"), [Barilla](/wiki/Barilla_Group "Barilla Group"), [Heinz](/wiki/H._J._Heinz_Company "H. J. Heinz Company"), Tone's Spices, [General Mills](/wiki/General_Mills "General Mills"), and [Quaker Oats](/wiki/Quaker_Oats "Quaker Oats"). Meatpacker [Tyson Foods](/wiki/Tyson_Foods "Tyson Foods") has 11 locations, second only to its headquarter state Arkansas.[[208]](#cite_note-213)

Major non-food manufacturing firms with production facilities in Iowa include [3M](/wiki/3M "3M"),[[209]](#cite_note-214) [Arconic](/wiki/Arconic "Arconic"),[[210]](#cite_note-215) [Amana Corporation](/wiki/Amana_Corporation "Amana Corporation"),[[211]](#cite_note-216) [Emerson Electric](/wiki/Emerson_Process_Management "Emerson Process Management"),[[212]](#cite_note-217) [The HON Company](/wiki/The_HON_Company "The HON Company"),[[213]](#cite_note-218) [SSAB](/wiki/SSAB "SSAB"),[[214]](#cite_note-219) [John Deere](/wiki/John_Deere "John Deere"),[[215]](#cite_note-220) [Lennox Manufacturing](/wiki/Lennox_International "Lennox International"),[[216]](#cite_note-221) [Pella Corporation](/wiki/Pella_(company) "Pella (company)"),[[217]](#cite_note-222) [Procter & Gamble](/wiki/Procter_%26_Gamble "Procter & Gamble"),[[218]](#cite_note-223) [Vermeer Company](/wiki/Vermeer_Company "Vermeer Company"),[[219]](#cite_note-224) and [Winnebago Industries](/wiki/Winnebago_Industries "Winnebago Industries").[[220]](#cite_note-225)

### Agriculture

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Iowa_harvest_2009.jpg/250px-Iowa_harvest_2009.jpg)](/wiki/File:Iowa_harvest_2009.jpg)

Harvesting [corn](/wiki/Maize "Maize") in [Jones County](/wiki/Jones_County,_Iowa "Jones County, Iowa")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Survivor%2C_Northwest_Iowa_7-13_%2822550505788%29.jpg/250px-Survivor%2C_Northwest_Iowa_7-13_%2822550505788%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Survivor,_Northwest_Iowa_7-13_(22550505788).jpg)

Farm in rural Northwest Iowa

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Maytag.jpg/250px-Maytag.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maytag.jpg)

Central Iowa cornfield and dairy in June

Industrial-scale, commodity agriculture predominates in much of the state. Iowa's main conventional agricultural commodities are [hogs](/wiki/Pig "Pig"), with about 22.6 million hogs in 8,000 facilities large enough to require manure management plans in March 2018, outnumbering Iowans by more than 7 to 1,[[221]](#cite_note-Jordan-226) [corn](/wiki/Maize "Maize"), [soybeans](/wiki/Soybean "Soybean"), [oats](/wiki/Oat "Oat"), cattle, eggs, and dairy products. Iowa is the nation's largest producer of [ethanol](/wiki/Ethanol "Ethanol") and [corn](/wiki/Maize "Maize") and some years is the largest grower of soybeans. In 2008, the 92,600 farms in Iowa produced 19% of the nation's corn, 17% of the soybeans, 30% of the hogs, and 14% of the eggs.[[222]](#cite_note-227)
As of 2009[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Iowa&action=edit) major Iowa agricultural product processors included [Archer Daniels Midland](/wiki/Archer_Daniels_Midland "Archer Daniels Midland"), [Cargill, Inc.](/wiki/Cargill,_Inc. "Cargill, Inc."), Diamond V Mills, and [Quaker Oats](/wiki/Quaker_Oats "Quaker Oats").[[223]](#cite_note-gazette_grain-228)

During the 21st century Iowa has seen growth in the [organic farming](/wiki/Organic_farming "Organic farming") sector. Iowa ranks fifth in the nation in total number of organic farms. In 2016, there were about 732 organic farms in the state, an increase of about 5% from the previous year, and 103,136 organic acres, an increase of 9,429 from the previous year.[[224]](#cite_note-229)[[225]](#cite_note-230) Iowa has also seen an increase in demand for local, sustainably-grown food. Northeast Iowa, part of the [Driftless Area](/wiki/Driftless_Area "Driftless Area"), has led the state in development of its regional [food system](/wiki/Food_system "Food system") and grows and consumes more local food than any other region in Iowa.[[226]](#cite_note-231)[[227]](#cite_note-232)

Iowa's Driftless Region is also home to the nationally recognized [Seed Savers Exchange](/wiki/Seed_Savers_Exchange "Seed Savers Exchange"), a non-profit [seed bank](/wiki/Seed_bank "Seed bank") housed at an 890-acre heritage farm near [Decorah](/wiki/Decorah,_Iowa "Decorah, Iowa"), in the northeast corner of the state.[[228]](#cite_note-233)[[229]](#cite_note-234) The largest nongovernmental seed bank of its kind in the United States, Seed Savers Exchange safeguards more than 20,000 varieties of rare, heirloom seeds.[[230]](#cite_note-235)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Wall_Mural%2C_Mt_Ayr%2C_IA_Post_Office%2C_1998.jpg/250px-Wall_Mural%2C_Mt_Ayr%2C_IA_Post_Office%2C_1998.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wall_Mural,_Mt_Ayr,_IA_Post_Office,_1998.jpg)

Mural in [Mt. Ayr](/wiki/Mount_Ayr,_Iowa "Mount Ayr, Iowa") Post Office, "The Corn Parade" by Orr C. Fischer, which was commissioned as part of the [New Deal](/wiki/New_Deal "New Deal")[[231]](#cite_note-CornParade-236)

As of 2007, the direct production and sale of conventional agricultural commodities contributed only about 3.5% of Iowa's gross state product.[[232]](#cite_note-237) In 2002 the impact of the indirect role of agriculture in Iowa's economy, including agriculture-affiliated business, was calculated at 16.4% in terms of value added and 24.3% in terms of total output. This was lower than the economic impact of non-farm manufacturing, which accounted for 22.4% of total value added and 26.5% of total output.[[233]](#cite_note-238)

### Health insurance

As of 2014, there were 16 organizations offering [health insurance](/wiki/Health_insurance "Health insurance") products in Iowa, per the State of Iowa Insurance Division.[[234]](#cite_note-iid-239)
Iowa was fourth out of ten states with the biggest drop in competition levels of health insurance between 2010 and 2011, per the 2013 annual report on the level of competition in the health insurance industry by the [American Medical Association](/wiki/American_Medical_Association "American Medical Association")[[235]](#cite_note-comp2013-240) using 2011 data from HealthLeaders-Interstudy, the most comprehensive source of data on enrollment in [health maintenance organization](/wiki/Health_maintenance_organization "Health maintenance organization") (HMO), [preferred provider organization](/wiki/Preferred_provider_organization "Preferred provider organization") (PPO), [point-of-service](/wiki/Point-of-service "Point-of-service") (POS) and [consumer-driven health care](/wiki/Consumer-driven_health_care "Consumer-driven health care") plans.[[236]](#cite_note-241)
According to the AMA annual report from 2007 [Wellmark Blue Cross Blue Shield](/wiki/Wellmark_Blue_Cross_Blue_Shield "Wellmark Blue Cross Blue Shield") had provided 71% of the state's health insurance.[[237]](#cite_note-242)

The Iowa Insurance Division "Annual report to the Iowa Governor and the Iowa Legislature" from November 2014 looked at the 95% of health insurers by premium, which are 10 companies. It found Wellmark Inc. to dominate the three health insurance markets it examined (individual, small group and large group) at 52–67%.[[238]](#cite_note-iidreport-243): 2 Wellmark HealthPlan of Iowa and Wellmark Inc had the highest risk-based capital percentages of all 10 providers at 1158% and 1132%, respectively.[[238]](#cite_note-iidreport-243): 31 Rising RBC is an indication of profits.[[238]](#cite_note-iidreport-243): 31

### Other sectors

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Ethanol_butler_co_iowa.jpg/250px-Ethanol_butler_co_iowa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ethanol_butler_co_iowa.jpg)

Ethanol plant under construction in [Butler County](/wiki/Butler_County,_Iowa "Butler County, Iowa")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Turbines_iowa.jpg/250px-Turbines_iowa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Turbines_iowa.jpg)

Wind turbines near [Williams, Iowa](/wiki/Williams,_Iowa "Williams, Iowa")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Iowa_electricity_production_by_type.webp/250px-Iowa_electricity_production_by_type.webp.png)](/wiki/File:Iowa_electricity_production_by_type.webp)

Iowa electricity production by type

Iowa has a strong financial and insurance sector, with approximately 6,100 firms,[[60]](#cite_note-iowalifechanging.com-61) including [AEGON](/wiki/Aegon_N.V. "Aegon N.V."), [Nationwide Group](/wiki/Nationwide_Mutual_Insurance_Company "Nationwide Mutual Insurance Company"), [Aviva USA](/wiki/Aviva "Aviva"), [Farm Bureau Financial Services](/wiki/Nationwide_Mutual_Insurance_Company "Nationwide Mutual Insurance Company"), [GreatAmerica Financial Services](/wiki/GreatAmerica_Financial_Services "GreatAmerica Financial Services"), [Voya Financial](/wiki/Voya_Financial "Voya Financial"), [Marsh Affinity Group](/wiki/Marsh_%26_McLennan_Companies "Marsh & McLennan Companies"), [MetLife](/wiki/MetLife "MetLife"), [Principal Financial Group](/wiki/Principal_Financial_Group "Principal Financial Group"), [Principal Capital Management](/wiki/Principal_Capital_Management "Principal Capital Management"), [Wells Fargo](/wiki/Wells_Fargo "Wells Fargo"), and Greenstate Credit Union (formerly University of Iowa Community Credit Union).

Iowa is host to at least two business incubators, Iowa State University Research Park and the BioVentures Center at the [University of Iowa](/wiki/University_of_Iowa "University of Iowa").[[239]](#cite_note-philipiddis2014-244) The Research Park hosts about 50 companies, among them NewLink Genetics, which develops [cancer immunotherapeutics](/wiki/Cancer_immunotherapy "Cancer immunotherapy"), and the U.S. [animal health](/wiki/Animal_health "Animal health") division of [Boehringer Ingelheim](/wiki/Boehringer_Ingelheim "Boehringer Ingelheim"), Vetmedica.[[239]](#cite_note-philipiddis2014-244)

[Ethanol](/wiki/Ethanol_fuel "Ethanol fuel") production consumes about a third of Iowa's corn production, and renewable fuels account for eight percent of the state's gross domestic product. A total of 39 ethanol plants produced 3.1 billion US gallons (12,000,000 m3) of fuel in 2009.[[240]](#cite_note-ethanol1-245)

Renewable energy has become a major economic force in northern and western Iowa, with [wind turbine](/wiki/Wind_turbine "Wind turbine") electrical generation increasing rapidly since 1990.[[14]](#cite_note-energy.iastate.edu-15) In 2019, [wind power in Iowa](/wiki/Wind_power_in_Iowa "Wind power in Iowa") accounted for 42% of electrical energy produced, and 10,201 megawatts of generating capacity had been installed at the end of the year.[[241]](#cite_note-eia_epm0217-246) Iowa ranked first of U.S. states in percentage of total power generated by wind and second in wind generating capacity behind Texas.[[241]](#cite_note-eia_epm0217-246) Major producers of turbines and components in Iowa include [Acciona Energy](/wiki/Acciona_Energy "Acciona Energy") of [West Branch](/wiki/West_Branch,_Iowa "West Branch, Iowa"), TPI Composites of [Newton](/wiki/Newton,_Iowa "Newton, Iowa"), and [Siemens](/wiki/Siemens "Siemens") Energy of [Fort Madison](/wiki/Fort_Madison "Fort Madison").

In 2016, Iowa was the headquarters for three of the top 2,000 companies for revenue.[[242]](#cite_note-247) They include [Principal Financial](/wiki/Principal_Financial "Principal Financial"), [Rockwell Collins](/wiki/Rockwell_Collins "Rockwell Collins"), and American Equity Investment.[[243]](#cite_note-248)[[244]](#cite_note-249)[[245]](#cite_note-250) Iowa is also headquarters to other companies including [Hy-Vee](/wiki/Hy-Vee "Hy-Vee"), [Pella Corporation](/wiki/Pella_(company) "Pella (company)"), [Workiva](/wiki/Workiva "Workiva"), [Vermeer Company](/wiki/Vermeer_Company "Vermeer Company"), [Kum & Go](/wiki/Kum_%26_Go "Kum & Go") gas stations, [Von Maur](/wiki/Von_Maur "Von Maur"), [Pioneer Hi-Bred](/wiki/Pioneer_Hi-Bred "Pioneer Hi-Bred"), and [Fareway](/wiki/Fareway "Fareway").[[246]](#cite_note-251)[[247]](#cite_note-252)[[248]](#cite_note-253)[[249]](#cite_note-254)[[250]](#cite_note-255)[[251]](#cite_note-256)[[252]](#cite_note-257)

[Gambling in the state](/wiki/Gambling_in_Iowa "Gambling in Iowa") is a major section of the Iowa tourism industry.[[253]](#cite_note-The-Daily-Iowan-2021-258)

As of 2025, Iowa had 64 [data centers](/wiki/Data_center "Data center"); Google in [Council Bluffs](/wiki/Council_Bluffs "Council Bluffs") and [Cedar Rapids](/wiki/Cedar_Rapids "Cedar Rapids"), Apple in [Waukee](/wiki/Waukee "Waukee"), Meta in [Altoona, Iowa](/wiki/Altoona,_Iowa "Altoona, Iowa") and Microsoft has six data centers in [West Des Moines](/wiki/West_Des_Moines "West Des Moines").[[254]](#cite_note-259)

### Taxation

Tax is collected by the [Iowa Department of Revenue](/wiki/Iowa_Department_of_Revenue "Iowa Department of Revenue").[[255]](#cite_note-260)

Iowa imposes taxes on net state income of individuals, estates, and trusts. There are nine [income tax](/wiki/Income_tax "Income tax") brackets, ranging from 0.36% to 8.98%, as well as four corporate income tax brackets ranging from 6% to 12%, giving Iowa the country's highest marginal corporate tax rate.[[256]](#cite_note-261) The state [sales tax](/wiki/Sales_tax "Sales tax") rate is 6%, with non-prepared food having no tax.[[257]](#cite_note-262) Iowa has one local option sales tax that may be imposed by counties after an election.[[258]](#cite_note-263) Property tax is levied on the taxable value of real property. Iowa has more than 2,000 taxing authorities. Most property is taxed by more than one taxing authority. The tax rate differs in each locality and is a composite of county, city or rural township, [school district](/wiki/School_district "School district") and special levies. Iowa allows its residents to deduct their [federal income taxes](/wiki/Federal_income_tax "Federal income tax") from their state income taxes.[[259]](#cite_note-264)

## Education

### Primary and secondary schools

See also: [List of school districts in Iowa](/wiki/List_of_school_districts_in_Iowa "List of school districts in Iowa") and [List of high schools in Iowa](/wiki/List_of_high_schools_in_Iowa "List of high schools in Iowa")

Iowa was one of the leading states in the [high school movement](/wiki/High_school_movement "High school movement"), and continues to be among the top educational performers today.[[260]](#cite_note-265)

The four-year graduation rate for high schoolers was 91.3% in 2017.[[261]](#cite_note-Grad_rate_2016-266) Iowa's schools are credited with the highest graduation rate in the nation as of 2019.[[262]](#cite_note-267) Iowa has 333 school districts,[[261]](#cite_note-Grad_rate_2016-266) 1,329 school buildings and has the 14th lowest student-to-teacher ratio of 14.2.[[263]](#cite_note-NEA-268) Teacher pay is ranked 22nd, with the average salary being $55,647.[[263]](#cite_note-NEA-268)

As of 2015[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Iowa&action=edit) transportation spending is a significant part of the budgets of rural school districts as many are geographically large and must transport students across vast distances. This reduces the amount of money spent on other aspects of the districts.[[264]](#cite_note-269)

The state's oldest functioning school building is located in [Bellevue](/wiki/Bellevue,_Iowa "Bellevue, Iowa") in the historic [Jackson County Courthouse](/wiki/Jackson_County_Courthouse_(Bellevue,_Iowa) "Jackson County Courthouse (Bellevue, Iowa)") which has been in continuous use as a school since 1848.[[265]](#cite_note-270)

### Colleges and universities

See also: [List of colleges and universities in Iowa](/wiki/List_of_colleges_and_universities_in_Iowa "List of colleges and universities in Iowa")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Palmer_Chiropractic_College.jpg/250px-Palmer_Chiropractic_College.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palmer_Chiropractic_College.jpg)

[Palmer Chiropractic College](/wiki/Palmer_Chiropractic_College "Palmer Chiropractic College") in [Davenport](/wiki/Davenport,_Iowa "Davenport, Iowa") is the first school of chiropractic in the world.

The [Iowa Board of Regents](/wiki/Board_of_Regents,_State_of_Iowa "Board of Regents, State of Iowa") is composed of nine citizen volunteers appointed by the governor to provide policymaking, coordination, and oversight of the state's three public universities, two special K–12 schools, and affiliated centers.[[266]](#cite_note-BoR-271)

The special K–12 schools include the [Iowa School for the Deaf](/wiki/Iowa_School_for_the_Deaf "Iowa School for the Deaf") in [Council Bluffs](/wiki/Council_Bluffs,_Iowa "Council Bluffs, Iowa") and the [Iowa Braille and Sight Saving School](/wiki/Iowa_Braille_and_Sight_Saving_School "Iowa Braille and Sight Saving School") in [Vinton](/wiki/Vinton,_Iowa "Vinton, Iowa").[[266]](#cite_note-BoR-271) Both [Iowa State University](/wiki/Iowa_State_University "Iowa State University") and [The University of Iowa](/wiki/The_University_of_Iowa "The University of Iowa") are research universities[[267]](#cite_note-272) with The University of Iowa also being a member of the [Association of American Universities](/wiki/Association_of_American_Universities "Association of American Universities").[[268]](#cite_note-273) In addition to the three state universities, Iowa has multiple private colleges and universities.

## Health

Main article: [Health in Iowa](/wiki/Health_in_Iowa "Health in Iowa")

The life expectancy of Iowa is 77.7 years in 2021.[[269]](#cite_note-Centers-for-Disease-Control-and-Prevention-2024-274) The state's rural population are more susceptible to diseases related to [food insecurity](/wiki/Food_security "Food security"), due to a lack of healthy food sources.[[270]](#cite_note-Smith-2024-275) The leading cause of death in Iowa is heart disease.[[269]](#cite_note-Centers-for-Disease-Control-and-Prevention-2024-274)

Iowa ranks eleventh among states in terms of proportion of obesity, with 37% of the state being affected by it.[[271]](#cite_note-Lensing-2024-276)[[272]](#cite_note-277) Around 9% of Iowans suffer from [diabetes](/wiki/Diabetes "Diabetes"), costing the state $2 billion.[[273]](#cite_note-278)[[274]](#cite_note-279)

## Transportation

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Iowa_overview.jpg/330px-Iowa_overview.jpg)](/wiki/File:Iowa_overview.jpg)

Iowa's major interstates, larger cities, and counties

### Interstate highways

Iowa has four primary [interstate highways](/wiki/Interstate_highways "Interstate highways"). [I-29](/wiki/Interstate_29_in_Iowa "Interstate 29 in Iowa") travels along the state's western edge through [Council Bluffs](/wiki/Council_Bluffs,_Iowa "Council Bluffs, Iowa") and [Sioux City](/wiki/Sioux_City,_Iowa "Sioux City, Iowa"). [I-35](/wiki/Interstate_35_in_Iowa "Interstate 35 in Iowa") travels from the Missouri state line to the Minnesota state line through the state's center, including Des Moines. [I-74](/wiki/Interstate_74_in_Iowa "Interstate 74 in Iowa") begins at [I-80](/wiki/Interstate_80_in_Iowa "Interstate 80 in Iowa") just northeast of [Davenport](/wiki/Davenport,_Iowa "Davenport, Iowa"). I-80 travels from the Nebraska state line to the Illinois state line through the center of the state, including Council Bluffs, Des Moines, [Iowa City](/wiki/Iowa_City,_Iowa "Iowa City, Iowa"), and the [Quad Cities](/wiki/Quad_Cities "Quad Cities"). [I-380](/wiki/Interstate_380_(Iowa) "Interstate 380 (Iowa)") is an [auxiliary Interstate Highway](/wiki/List_of_auxiliary_Interstate_Highways "List of auxiliary Interstate Highways"), which travels from I-80 near Iowa City through [Cedar Rapids](/wiki/Cedar_Rapids,_Iowa "Cedar Rapids, Iowa") ending in [Waterloo](/wiki/Waterloo,_Iowa "Waterloo, Iowa") and is part of the [Avenue of the Saints](/wiki/Avenue_of_the_Saints "Avenue of the Saints") highway. Iowa is among the few jurisdictions where municipalities install speed cameras on interstate highways providing a substantial revenue source from out of state drivers.[[275]](#cite_note-280)

### Airports with scheduled flights

See also: [List of airports in Iowa](/wiki/List_of_airports_in_Iowa "List of airports in Iowa")

Iowa is served by several regional airports including the [Des Moines International Airport](/wiki/Des_Moines_International_Airport "Des Moines International Airport"), the [Eastern Iowa Airport](/wiki/Eastern_Iowa_Airport "Eastern Iowa Airport"), in Cedar Rapids, [Quad City International Airport](/wiki/Quad_City_International_Airport "Quad City International Airport"), in [Moline, Illinois](/wiki/Moline,_Illinois "Moline, Illinois"), and [Eppley Airfield](/wiki/Eppley_Airfield "Eppley Airfield"), in [Omaha, Nebraska](/wiki/Omaha,_Nebraska "Omaha, Nebraska"). Smaller airports in the state include the [Council Bluffs Municipal Airport](/wiki/Council_Bluffs_Municipal_Airport "Council Bluffs Municipal Airport"), [Davenport Municipal Airport (Iowa)](/wiki/Davenport_Municipal_Airport_(Iowa) "Davenport Municipal Airport (Iowa)"), [Dubuque Regional Airport](/wiki/Dubuque_Regional_Airport "Dubuque Regional Airport"), [Fort Dodge Regional Airport](/wiki/Fort_Dodge_Regional_Airport "Fort Dodge Regional Airport"), [Mason City Municipal Airport](/wiki/Mason_City_Municipal_Airport "Mason City Municipal Airport"), [Sioux Gateway Airport](/wiki/Sioux_Gateway_Airport "Sioux Gateway Airport"), [Southeast Iowa Regional Airport](/wiki/Southeast_Iowa_Regional_Airport "Southeast Iowa Regional Airport"), and [Waterloo Regional Airport](/wiki/Waterloo_Regional_Airport "Waterloo Regional Airport").[[276]](#cite_note-281)

### Railroads

See also: [List of Iowa railroads](/wiki/List_of_Iowa_railroads "List of Iowa railroads")

[Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak")'s *[California Zephyr](/wiki/California_Zephyr "California Zephyr")* serves southern Iowa with stops in [Burlington](/wiki/Burlington,_Iowa "Burlington, Iowa"), [Mount Pleasant](/wiki/Mount_Pleasant,_Iowa "Mount Pleasant, Iowa"), [Ottumwa](/wiki/Ottumwa,_Iowa "Ottumwa, Iowa"), [Osceola](/wiki/Osceola,_Iowa "Osceola, Iowa"), and [Creston](/wiki/Creston,_Iowa "Creston, Iowa") along its route between Chicago and [Emeryville, California](/wiki/Emeryville,_California "Emeryville, California").[[277]](#cite_note-282) [Fort Madison](/wiki/Fort_Madison,_Iowa "Fort Madison, Iowa") is served by Amtrak's *[Southwest Chief](/wiki/Southwest_Chief "Southwest Chief")*, running between [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") and [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles").[[278]](#cite_note-283) Both provide daily service through the state.

### Public Transit

Iowa is served by a number of local transit providers including [Bettendorf Transit](/wiki/Bettendorf_Transit "Bettendorf Transit"), [Cambus](/wiki/Cambus "Cambus"), [Cedar Rapids Transit](/wiki/Cedar_Rapids_Transit "Cedar Rapids Transit"), [Clinton Municipal Transit Administration](/wiki/Clinton_Municipal_Transit_Administration "Clinton Municipal Transit Administration"), [Coralville Transit](/wiki/Coralville_Transit "Coralville Transit"), [Cyride](/wiki/Cyride "Cyride"), [Davenport Citibus](/wiki/Davenport_Citibus "Davenport Citibus"), [Des Moines Area Regional Transit](/wiki/Des_Moines_Area_Regional_Transit "Des Moines Area Regional Transit"), [Iowa City Transit](/wiki/Iowa_City_Transit "Iowa City Transit"), [The Jule](/wiki/The_Jule "The Jule"), [Mason City Transit](/wiki/Mason_City_Transit "Mason City Transit"), [MET Transit](/wiki/Metropolitan_Transit_Authority_of_Black_Hawk_County "Metropolitan Transit Authority of Black Hawk County"), [Omaha Metro Transit](/wiki/Metro_Transit_(Omaha) "Metro Transit (Omaha)"), [Ottumwa Transit Authority](/wiki/Ottumwa_Transit_Authority "Ottumwa Transit Authority"), [Quad Cities MetroLINK](/wiki/Quad_Cities_MetroLINK "Quad Cities MetroLINK") and [Sioux City Transit](/wiki/Sioux_City_Transit "Sioux City Transit").

Intercity bus service in the state is provided by [Burlington Trailways](/wiki/Burlington_Trailways "Burlington Trailways"), [Greyhound Lines](/wiki/Greyhound_Lines "Greyhound Lines"), and [Jefferson Lines](/wiki/Jefferson_Lines "Jefferson Lines").

## Law and government

### State

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Iowa_State_Capitol_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Iowa_State_Capitol_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Iowa_State_Capitol_-_panoramio.jpg)

The [Iowa State Capitol](/wiki/Iowa_State_Capitol "Iowa State Capitol") in Des Moines, completed in 1886, is the only state capitol in the United States to feature five domes. It houses the [Iowa General Assembly](/wiki/Iowa_General_Assembly "Iowa General Assembly"), comprising the [Iowa House of Representatives](/wiki/Iowa_House_of_Representatives "Iowa House of Representatives") and [Iowa Senate](/wiki/Iowa_Senate "Iowa Senate").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Iowa_Supreme_Court.jpg/250px-Iowa_Supreme_Court.jpg)](/wiki/File:Iowa_Supreme_Court.jpg)

The [Iowa Supreme Court](/wiki/Iowa_Supreme_Court "Iowa Supreme Court"), across from the capitol, is the state's highest court.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/State_of_Iowa_logo.svg/250px-State_of_Iowa_logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:State_of_Iowa_logo.svg)

Logo for the State of Iowa.

As of 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Iowa&action=edit), the 43rd and current [governor of Iowa](/wiki/Governor_of_Iowa "Governor of Iowa") is [Kim Reynolds](/wiki/Kim_Reynolds "Kim Reynolds") (R). Other statewide elected officials are:

* [Chris Cournoyer](/wiki/Chris_Cournoyer "Chris Cournoyer") (R), [Lieutenant Governor](/wiki/List_of_lieutenant_governors_of_Iowa "List of lieutenant governors of Iowa")[[279]](#cite_note-284)
* [Paul Pate](/wiki/Paul_Pate "Paul Pate") (R), [Secretary of State](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_of_Iowa "Secretary of State of Iowa")
* [Rob Sand](/wiki/Rob_Sand "Rob Sand") (D), [Auditor of State](/wiki/Auditor_of_State_of_Iowa "Auditor of State of Iowa")
* [Roby Smith](/wiki/Roby_Smith "Roby Smith") (R), [Treasurer of State](/wiki/Treasurer_of_State_of_Iowa "Treasurer of State of Iowa")
* [Mike Naig](/wiki/Mike_Naig "Mike Naig") (R), [Secretary of Agriculture](/wiki/Secretary_of_Agriculture_of_Iowa "Secretary of Agriculture of Iowa")
* [Brenna Bird](/wiki/Brenna_Bird "Brenna Bird") (R), [Attorney General](/wiki/Attorney_General_of_Iowa "Attorney General of Iowa")

The [Code of Iowa](/wiki/Code_of_Iowa "Code of Iowa") contains Iowa's [statutory laws](/wiki/Statutory_law "Statutory law"). It is periodically updated by the Iowa Legislative Service Bureau, with a new edition published in odd-numbered years and a supplement published in even-numbered years.

Iowa is an alcohol monopoly or [alcoholic beverage control state](/wiki/Alcoholic_beverage_control_state "Alcoholic beverage control state").

### National

Further information: [List of United States senators from Iowa](/wiki/List_of_United_States_senators_from_Iowa "List of United States senators from Iowa") and [List of United States representatives from Iowa](/wiki/List_of_United_States_representatives_from_Iowa "List of United States representatives from Iowa")

The two U.S. senators:

* [Chuck Grassley](/wiki/Chuck_Grassley "Chuck Grassley") (R), in office since 1981
* [Joni Ernst](/wiki/Joni_Ernst "Joni Ernst") (R), in office since 2015

The four U.S. representatives:

* [Mariannette Miller-Meeks](/wiki/Mariannette_Miller-Meeks "Mariannette Miller-Meeks") (R), [First district](/wiki/Iowa%27s_1st_congressional_district "Iowa's 1st congressional district")
* [Ashley Hinson](/wiki/Ashley_Hinson "Ashley Hinson") (R), [Second district](/wiki/Iowa%27s_2nd_congressional_district "Iowa's 2nd congressional district")
* [Zach Nunn](/wiki/Zach_Nunn "Zach Nunn") (R), [Third district](/wiki/Iowa%27s_3rd_congressional_district "Iowa's 3rd congressional district")
* [Randy Feenstra](/wiki/Randy_Feenstra "Randy Feenstra") (R), [Fourth district](/wiki/Iowa%27s_4th_congressional_district "Iowa's 4th congressional district")

After the [2010 United States census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census") and the resulting redistricting, Iowa lost one seat in Congress, falling to four seats in the U.S. House of Representatives. Incumbent U.S. representatives [Leonard Boswell](/wiki/Leonard_Boswell "Leonard Boswell") (D) and [Tom Latham](/wiki/Tom_Latham_(politician) "Tom Latham (politician)") (R) ran against each other in [2012](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives_elections_in_Iowa,_2012 "United States House of Representatives elections in Iowa, 2012") in the third congressional district which had new boundaries; Latham won and retired after the [2014 elections](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives_elections_in_Iowa,_2014 "United States House of Representatives elections in Iowa, 2014"). King represented the old [fifth congressional district](/wiki/Iowa%27s_5th_congressional_district "Iowa's 5th congressional district").

### Political parties

Main article: [Political party strength in Iowa](/wiki/Political_party_strength_in_Iowa "Political party strength in Iowa")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Samuel_Jordan_Kirkwood.jpg/250px-Samuel_Jordan_Kirkwood.jpg)](/wiki/File:Samuel_Jordan_Kirkwood.jpg)

[Samuel J. Kirkwood](/wiki/Samuel_J._Kirkwood "Samuel J. Kirkwood"), founder of the Iowa Republican Party, abolitionist, and Iowa's Civil War Governor

In Iowa, the term "political party" refers to political organizations which have received two percent or more of the votes cast for president or governor in the "last preceding general election". Iowa recognizes three political parties—the [Republican Party](/wiki/Republican_Party_of_Iowa "Republican Party of Iowa"), the [Democratic Party](/wiki/Iowa_Democratic_Party "Iowa Democratic Party"), and the [Libertarian Party](/wiki/Libertarian_Party_(United_States) "Libertarian Party (United States)").[[280]](#cite_note-285) The Libertarian Party obtained official political party status in 2017 as a result of presidential candidate [Gary Johnson](/wiki/Gary_Johnson "Gary Johnson") receiving 3.8% of the Iowa vote in the [2016 general election](/wiki/2016_United_States_presidential_election "2016 United States presidential election").[[281]](#cite_note-286) [Third parties](/wiki/Third_party_(politics) "Third party (politics)"), officially termed "nonparty political organizations", can appear on the ballot as well. Four of these have had candidates on the ballot in Iowa since 2004 for various positions: the [Constitution Party](/wiki/Constitution_Party_(United_States) "Constitution Party (United States)"), the [Green Party](/wiki/Green_Party_(United_States) "Green Party (United States)"), the [Pirate Party](/wiki/United_States_Pirate_Party "United States Pirate Party"), and the [Socialist Workers Party](/wiki/Socialist_Workers_Party_(United_States) "Socialist Workers Party (United States)").[[282]](#cite_note-287)[[283]](#cite_note-288)

### Voter trends

See also: [United States presidential elections in Iowa](/wiki/United_States_presidential_elections_in_Iowa "United States presidential elections in Iowa")

As a result of the 2010 elections, each party controlled one house of the [Iowa General Assembly](/wiki/Iowa_General_Assembly "Iowa General Assembly"): the House had a Republican majority, while the Senate had a Democratic majority. As a result of the 2016 elections, Republicans gained control of the Senate. Incumbent Democratic governor [Chet Culver](/wiki/Chet_Culver "Chet Culver") was defeated in 2010 by Republican [Terry Branstad](/wiki/Terry_Branstad "Terry Branstad"), who had served as governor from 1983 to 1999. On December 14, 2015, Branstad became the longest serving governor in U.S. history, serving (at that time) 20 years, 11 months, and 3 days; eclipsing [George Clinton](/wiki/George_Clinton_(vice_president) "George Clinton (vice president)"), who served 21 years until 1804.[[284]](#cite_note-289) Lieutenant Governor Kim Reynolds succeeded him on May 24, 2017, following Branstad's appointment as U.S. Ambassador to China.

In previous eras, Democratic and Republican parties had a balance in state politics and federal representation. By 2024, the two became dominated by the Republican Party. Factors include younger people leaving for more urbanized, more Democratic-leaning states, as well as homogenization of rural voters in the Midwest and in other regions.[[285]](#cite_note-290)

### Presidential caucus

Main article: [Iowa caucuses](/wiki/Iowa_caucuses "Iowa caucuses")

The state gets considerable attention every four years because the [Iowa caucus](/wiki/Iowa_caucus "Iowa caucus"), gatherings of voters to select delegates to the state conventions, is the first presidential caucus in the country. The caucuses, held in January or February of the election year, involve people gathering in homes or public places and choosing their candidates, rather than casting secret ballots as is done in a [presidential primary](/wiki/Presidential_primary "Presidential primary") election.[[287]](#cite_note-292)
Along with the [New Hampshire primary](/wiki/New_Hampshire_primary "New Hampshire primary") the following week, Iowa's caucuses have become the starting points for choosing the two major-party candidates for president.[[288]](#cite_note-293) The national and international media give Iowa and New Hampshire extensive attention, which gives Iowa voters leverage.[[289]](#cite_note-294) In 2007 presidential campaign spending was the seventh highest in the country.[[290]](#cite_note-295)

### Civil rights

In a 2020 study, Iowa was ranked as the 24th easiest state for citizens to vote in.[[291]](#cite_note-296)

#### Racial equality

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/4/4f/UnionBlockMtPleasant15.jpg/250px-UnionBlockMtPleasant15.jpg)](/wiki/File:UnionBlockMtPleasant15.jpg)

The [Union Block](/wiki/Union_Block_(Mount_Pleasant,_Iowa) "Union Block (Mount Pleasant, Iowa)") building in [Mount Pleasant](/wiki/Mount_Pleasant,_Iowa "Mount Pleasant, Iowa"), scene of early civil rights and women's rights activities

In the 19th century Iowa was among the earliest states to enact prohibitions against race discrimination, especially in education, but was slow to achieve full integration in the 20th century. In the first decision of the [Iowa Supreme Court](/wiki/Iowa_Supreme_Court "Iowa Supreme Court")—*In Re the Matter of Ralph*,[[292]](#cite_note-297) decided July 1839—the Court rejected slavery in a decision that found a slave named Ralph became free when he stepped on Iowa soil, 26 years before the end of the Civil War.[[293]](#cite_note-civil_rights-298) The state did away with racial barriers to marriage in 1851, more than 100 years before the U.S. Supreme Court would ban [miscegenation statutes](/wiki/Anti-miscegenation_laws "Anti-miscegenation laws") nationwide.[[294]](#cite_note-ChicagoTrib-299)

The Iowa Supreme Court decided *Clark v. The board of directors*[[295]](#cite_note-300) in 1868, ruling that racially segregated "separate but equal" schools had no place in Iowa, 85 years before *[Brown v. Board of Education](/wiki/Brown_v._Board_of_Education "Brown v. Board of Education")*.[[293]](#cite_note-civil_rights-298) By 1875, a number of additional court rulings effectively ended segregation in Iowa schools.[[296]](#cite_note-301) Social and housing discrimination continued against Blacks at state universities until the 1950s.[[297]](#cite_note-302) The Court heard *Coger v. The North Western Union Packet Co.*[[298]](#cite_note-303) in 1873, ruling against racial discrimination in public accommodations 91 years before the U.S. Supreme Court reached the same decision.[[293]](#cite_note-civil_rights-298)

In 1884, the Iowa Civil Rights Act apparently outlawed discrimination by businesses, reading: "All persons within this state shall be entitled to the full and equal enjoyment of the accommodations, advantages, facilities, and privileges of inns, restaurants, chophouses, eating houses, lunch counters, and all other places where refreshments are served, public conveyances, barber shops, bathhouses, theaters, and all other places of amusement." However, the courts chose to narrowly apply this act, allowing de facto discrimination to continue.[[299]](#cite_note-304) Racial discrimination at public businesses was not deemed illegal until 1949, when the court ruled in *[State of Iowa v. Katz](/wiki/State_of_Iowa_v._Katz "State of Iowa v. Katz")* that businesses had to serve customers regardless of race; the case began when [Edna Griffin](/wiki/Edna_Griffin "Edna Griffin") was denied service at a Des Moines drugstore.[[300]](#cite_note-305) Full racial civil rights were codified under the Iowa Civil Rights Act of 1965.[[301]](#cite_note-306)

#### Women's rights

As with racial equality, Iowa was a vanguard in women's rights in the mid-19th century, but did not allow women the right to vote until the [Nineteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution](/wiki/Nineteenth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Nineteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution") was ratified in 1920, Iowa legislature being one of the ratifying votes.[[302]](#cite_note-nps-307) In 1847, the [University of Iowa](/wiki/University_of_Iowa "University of Iowa") became the first public university in the U.S. to admit men and women on an equal basis.[[303]](#cite_note-308) In 1869, Iowa became the first state in the union to admit women to the practice of law, with the Court ruling women may not be denied the right to practice law in Iowa and admitting [Arabella A. Mansfield](/wiki/Arabella_A._Mansfield "Arabella A. Mansfield") to the practice of law.[[293]](#cite_note-civil_rights-298) Several attempts to grant full voting rights to Iowa women were defeated between 1870 and 1919. In 1894 women were given "partial suffrage", which allowed them to vote on issues, but not for candidates. It was not until the ratification of the Nineteenth Amendment that women had full suffrage in Iowa.[[304]](#cite_note-309) Although Iowa supported the Federal [Equal Rights Amendment](/wiki/Equal_Rights_Amendment "Equal Rights Amendment"), in 1980 and 1992 Iowa voters rejected an Equal Rights Amendment to the state constitution.[[305]](#cite_note-310) Iowa added the word "women" to the Iowa Constitution in 1998. After the amendment, it reads: "All men and women are, by nature, free and equal, and have certain inalienable rights—among which are those of enjoying and defending life and liberty, acquiring, possessing and protecting property, and pursuing and obtaining safety and happiness."[[306]](#cite_note-311)

In May 2018 Iowa signed into law one of the country's most restrictive abortion bans: the requirement that a doctor cannot perform an abortion if they can detect a fetal heartbeat, which in many cases would restrict abortions pregnancies less than six weeks old.[[307]](#cite_note-312) It was struck down in January 2019, when an Iowa state judge ruled that the "fetal heartbeat" law was unconstitutional.[[308]](#cite_note-313)

#### LGBTQ rights

Main article: [LGBTQ rights in Iowa](/wiki/LGBTQ_rights_in_Iowa "LGBTQ rights in Iowa")

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article needs to be **updated**. The reason given is: **update with 2020s anti-transgender legislation including [Iowa Senate File 538](/wiki/Iowa_Senate_File_538 "Iowa Senate File 538") and [Senate File 418](/wiki/Senate_File_418 "Senate File 418")**. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(June 2025)* |

The state's law criminalizing same-sex sexual activity was repealed in June 1976, 27 years before *[Lawrence v. Texas](/wiki/Lawrence_v._Texas "Lawrence v. Texas")*. In 2007, the Iowa Legislature added "sexual orientation" and "gender identity" to the protected classes listed in the Iowa Civil Rights Act.[[309]](#cite_note-314) The Iowa Legislature later struck "gender identity" from these protections in 2025, making Iowa the first U.S. state to remove a [protected group](/wiki/Protected_group "Protected group") from a state [anti-discrimination law](/wiki/Anti-discrimination_law "Anti-discrimination law").[[310]](#cite_note-315)

On April 3, 2009, the Iowa Supreme Court decided *[Varnum v. Brien](/wiki/Varnum_v._Brien "Varnum v. Brien")*,[[311]](#cite_note-316) holding in a [unanimous](/wiki/Unanimous "Unanimous") decision,[[312]](#cite_note-317) the state's law forbidding [same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage "Same-sex marriage") was [unconstitutional](/wiki/Unconstitutional "Unconstitutional"). This made Iowa the third state in the U.S. and first in the Midwest to permit same-sex marriage.[[313]](#cite_note-318)[[314]](#cite_note-319)
